actions.thought: Holding Ace-King offsuit is a premium hand, representing one of the strongest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Being on the button gives me the advantage of position for the rest of the hand. A standard opening raise of 3x the big blind is appropriate to build the pot and take the initiative against the blinds.

98 offsuit is a weak hand to defend from the small blind against a button raise. Being out of position with a marginal holding will lead to losing money long term. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Big Blind holding 8s 4h. The Button (Flashlight) raised to 3x the blind. 84o is a very weak hand with poor playability and equity, especially out of position. Even though I am getting roughly 2.25:1 odds to call, defending such a weak hand against a standard opening range is generally a losing play in the long term. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

J8s is a decent suited one-gapper. Being on the button in a 3-handed game, this hand is certainly strong enough to open. Raising to 2.5x the big blind applies pressure to the blinds and allows me to take the initiative into the pot, maintaining a positional advantage post-flop.

I am holding 72o, which is statistically the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em. I am in the small blind and facing a raise from the button. Even though the pot odds are 3:1, 72o performs very poorly post-flop, especially out of position. There is no reason to defend this hand.

I hold a weak offsuit hand, J8o, in the Big Blind. While I am getting reasonable pot odds (150 to win 400) to call the raise, the hand plays poorly post-flop, and I am out of position against the button. Given this is the early stage of the tournament, I prefer to fold and preserve my stack for a more advantageous situation rather than playing a marginal hand out of position.

In 3-handed play, T7o is a marginal hand that sits at the very bottom of a standard button opening range. While an aggressive player might open this to steal the blinds, it frequently plays poorly post-flop and can lead to difficult spots against the blinds. Given that I have folded my previous two hands and the session is in its early stages, I will continue to play a disciplined game and fold this hand to wait for a stronger opening opportunity.

I am in the Small Blind with a very weak hand, 4-5 offsuit. Since the Button has folded, I am heads-up against the Big Blind. Playing this hand out of position is negative expected value, so the most disciplined action is to fold and preserve my stack for a more favorable situation.

In a 3-handed game, the button position is advantageous, and ten-six suited is a playable hand for a late-position steal attempt against the small and big blinds. Raising allows me to take control of the pot and potentially win the blinds immediately, given the wide ranges expected in a 3-max format.
2s6c is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Facing a raise from the Button, folding from the Small Blind is the only sensible choice to preserve chips.

I am in the Big Blind holding J7o. The Button (Flashlight) has opened to 300, which is a 3x raise. J7o is a weak hand that does not play well out of position. Although my pot odds are roughly 2.25:1, the lack of connectivity and the disadvantage of being out of position for the remainder of the hand makes this a clear fold in a three-handed game at this stack depth.

J6 suited is a marginal hand even 3-handed on the button. While it has some steal equity and can flop flush draws, it suffers from reverse implied odds when making a top pair of Jacks (bad kicker) or a weak pair of 6s. It's better to preserve the stack for a stronger starting hand with better playability.

Pro folded on the button, leaving just the blinds. I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Four suited (Qd4d). This hand is strong enough to attempt a steal against the Big Blind in a 3-handed game. I will raise to 300 to put pressure on Flashlight and try to take down the blinds pre-flop. If called, the suited nature of the hand gives me some playability on the flop.

Holding 3s 7s in the big blind against a raise from the small blind, I am facing a bet of 200 to potentially win a total pot of 400. While the price is enticing, 37s is a weak hand that will rarely flop well enough to continue comfortably. Given the early stage of the tournament, it is more prudent to fold and wait for a more advantageous spot than to play a marginal, dominated hand.

I am on the button with Ace-Jack suited (Ac Jc), which is a premier starting hand in a 3-handed game. My goal is to play for value and exert pressure from position. I will open with a standard 3x raise to 300 to build the pot and potentially take down the blinds immediately.

Holding 5d 4c in the small blind against a button raise is extremely weak. Being out of position with a marginal hand makes it difficult to realize equity. Folding is the most disciplined and +EV choice here to preserve stack size for better situations.
54o is a very weak and unconnected hand. Even though we are defending the Big Blind against a Button open, 54o is too weak to call a 3x raise out of position. I will fold and save my chips.

I am on the button in a 3-handed game with 4s 6s. This is a suited connector, which plays well in position. Opening the pot from the button is a standard strategy to steal the blinds and take control of the hand. A raise to 300 is a solid sizing to pressure the blinds, given the current stack depths.

I am in the Small Blind with 7c 5d, which is a very weak and disconnected offsuit hand. Flashlight raised to 3x the big blind from the Button. Being out of position with a trash hand, this is a clear fold.

I am in the Big Blind with 7d 6d, a strong suited connector. Flashlight (Button) raised to 300, and the Small Blind folded. I need to call 200 more to see a flop in a pot that will be 650. 76s has excellent playability and implied odds in deep-stacked situations. While I will be out of position for the rest of the hand, the pot odds and the hand's potential to hit straights or flushes make this a clear defense of the big blind. I will call and see a flop.

The flop of Qc-Ks-Tc is very dynamic and connects strongly with the pre-flop raiser's range, which likely includes many Broadway cards and pairs. I have no pair, no draw, and no backdoor equity with my 7d 6d. Betting into the aggressor on this board is ill-advised. I will check and most likely fold if faced with a bet.

The flop is extremely coordinated with Qc-Ks-Tc, which connects very well with the big blind's defending range. My hand (4s-6s) has no pair and no draw, providing zero equity on this board. Since my opponent checked, checking behind is the optimal play to maintain control of the pot size and avoid overcommitting to a bluff on a board that is highly likely to have hit the opponent. I will take the free card and re-evaluate on the turn.

The turn card is a complete brick (4h) and does not improve my hand. I still have 7-high on a very coordinated board (Qc-Ks-Tc) that connects heavily with my opponent's opening range from the button. Since both players checked the flop, I have no reason to start betting now with zero equity and no draws. I will check and likely fold to any bet, as I have no showdown value.

The board texture is highly connected with broadway cards (K-Q-T). Holding a pair of fours, I have a marginal showdown hand, but it is extremely vulnerable to any higher card or straight/flush draws. My opponent has checked twice, suggesting they are either playing a weak hand passively or trapping. Given the board texture and my weak kicker, betting would likely only be called by better hands that I cannot beat. Checking behind allows me to control the pot size, take a free look at the river, and avoid bloating a pot with a hand that likely won't hold up in a showdown if there is significant aggression.

On the river, I have improved to bottom pair (7s). The board is extremely coordinated (Q-K-T) and hits a button raising range hard. However, the pre-flop aggressor (Flashlight) has checked both the flop and the turn, indicating they likely do not have a strong hand like a straight, set, or two pair. While my pair of sevens is weak, it has some showdown value in a pot that has been checked down. There is no merit in betting here, as I would only be called by better hands. I will check and hope to see a cheap showdown or fold if a large bet is made, though a small bet might be worth a crying call depending on the odds.

I have reached the river with only a pair of fours on a highly connected board (Qc-Ks-Tc-4h-7h). My hand is extremely weak and unlikely to beat a calling range. Since my opponent has checked, there is no reason to bet as I will only be called by better hands. Checking behind allows me to take my weak showdown value to the end without risking any more chips.

With pocket threes on the button in a 3-handed game, I have a hand that is strong enough to open-raise. A raise to 2.5x the big blind (250 chips) puts pressure on the blinds to fold, potentially winning the pot outright, while also giving me the initiative and positional advantage if called. If I get called, I'll be looking to flop a set or outplay my opponents post-flop.

I am in the Small Blind with 2d 4d, a very weak suited hand. The Button (Pro) has raised to 250. Calling out of position with such low cards is generally a losing play in the long run, as I will frequently be dominated or forced to fold on most flops. While the suitedness adds some value, 2-4 is too low to reliably contest this pot against a Button open. I will fold to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am in the big blind with Ace-Eight suited, a solid hand to defend against a button open. With 96 big blinds remaining, I have plenty of stack depth to play post-flop. The pot odds are favorable (150 to call a 400 total pot, effectively giving me roughly 27% equity requirement), and folding here would be too tight given that the button likely has a wide opening range. I prefer calling over 3-betting to avoid bloating the pot out of position against a potentially stronger range that could 4-bet me off my equity.

I am in the big blind with the nut flush draw on a Jack-high board. Since I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, checking is the standard strategic play to evaluate the opponent's continuation bet or to realize my equity if they check back.

With pocket threes on a wet board of Js 9s 5c, my hand is very vulnerable. Flashlight's big blind calling range hits this board quite well with plenty of jacks, nines, flush draws, and straight draws. A continuation bet is unlikely to get folds from better hands or strong draws, and I have very little equity if called. Checking behind allows me to control the size of the pot, keep the range wide, and try to realize my small amount of equity for free.

With the nut flush draw and an open-ended straight draw on this coordinated board (Js-9s-5c-Th), I possess significant equity. Given that the flop checked through, betting now serves as a strong semi-bluff that can take down the pot immediately or build value for when I improve on the river. This size (350 into 550) is substantial enough to apply pressure while giving me a good price to realize my equity.

My pocket threes are practically worthless on this wet and coordinated board (Js 9s 5c Th). Flashlight's lead on the turn indicates strength or a solid semi-bluff that still has massive equity against my underpair. I have no realistic draws to improve, and even if I hit a 3 on the river, there's a strong possibility of a straight already out there. Calling here is burning chips; folding is the only correct play.

Qc 7c is a standard opening hand from the button in a 3-handed game. I have position and a hand with decent post-flop playability. Raising to 2.5x BB is a standard play to put pressure on the blinds and build the pot.

Holding 5-6 offsuit in the Small Blind against a button raise is a clear fold. The hand has poor playability, is out of position, and there is no compelling reason to invest chips into a weak hand this early in a deep-stack tournament structure. It is better to wait for a stronger starting hand or a better situational opportunity.

I am in the Big Blind with 6s5d. The Button has raised to 250, and the Small Blind folded. While I'm getting decent pot odds to call (150 to win 400) and close the action, 65o is a very weak hand that plays poorly out of position post-flop. The risk of being dominated or playing out of position without a strong draw is too high. Folding is the most disciplined and profitable decision.

Qd-6d is a weak hand to open with in a three-handed game. While being on the button grants me positional advantage, the hand strength is insufficient to warrant an open raise. It is better to fold and wait for a more advantageous spot to enter the pot.

Ten-Five offsuit is a very weak starting hand. Being out of position in the small blind against the big blind makes it even worse. The standard and optimal play is to fold here and save the chips for a better spot.
84s is too weak of a hand to play from the button even in a 3-handed game. Folding is the standard and optimal play here to preserve chips.

I have pocket fives in the small blind after the button has folded. In a blind vs. blind situation, a pocket pair is a strong starting hand. I will raise to 300 to take control of the pot and potentially win it immediately or set up a strong position for post-flop play against the big blind.

I am holding 9-2 offsuit in the big blind, which is one of the weakest possible hands in Texas Hold'em. While the pot odds are mathematically tempting, playing such a disconnected, low-equity hand out of position against an opponent's opening range is negative expected value. It is much more prudent to fold and preserve my stack for a better starting hand.

I am on the button with 2d 6s, which is an extremely weak hand. While the button is a good position for stealing blinds, this specific hand has very little playability or equity if called. Given my current stack lead, there is no need to take unnecessary risks with such poor holdings. I will fold.

I am in the small blind and the button has folded. With Ace-Eight offsuit, I have a strong enough hand to raise against the big blind to try and take the pot immediately or play with the betting lead. A raise to 300 is a standard size for this stack depth.

We hold pocket sevens in the Big Blind facing a Small Blind raise. Blind versus blind, the Small Blind's raising range is typically quite wide, meaning our 77 is often ahead. While a 3-bet is an option to deny equity, flatting allows us to keep his weaker hands in the pot, control the size of the pot, and utilize our positional advantage post-flop. It also disguises our hand strength and gives us good implied odds to set-mine.

I raised pre-flop from the small blind and the big blind called. The flop of Tc-6d-5c is somewhat dry but connects well with a calling range. Although I have missed the flop completely with Ace-high, I am the aggressor. A continuation bet of 300 (half the pot) allows me to represent strength and potentially take down the pot immediately, or gather information about the opponent's holding while maintaining the initiative.

On a Tc 6d 5c flop, we hold second pair with pocket sevens. The Small Blind's continuation bet of half pot is standard and will include many overcards, flush draws, and straight draws that we are currently ahead of. Our hand is too strong to fold, but raising would likely isolate us against a tighter range that beats us (like Tx or overpairs) while folding out the bluffs we want to keep in the pot. Calling allows us to control the pot size, maintain our positional advantage, and evaluate on the turn.

The turn 9h makes the board more connected, potentially helping ranges that include 7-8 or other mid-connectors. Since my continuation bet on the flop was called, it is unlikely my Ace-high has significant fold equity against a calling range that likely includes at least a pair or a strong draw. Checking is the most prudent play to control the pot size and avoid bluffing into a made hand, as I have no significant equity to improve.

On the turn, the 9h adds another overcard to our pocket sevens and makes the board more connected. Flashlight's check could indicate that he has given up with some of his bluffs like overcards or missed draws, or he is pot-controlling a hand like a weak Ten or a Nine. With third pair and a gutshot straight draw, we have marginal showdown value but aren't strong enough to value bet, and turning our hand into a bluff here isn't ideal because better hands likely won't fold. Checking behind allows us to realize our equity, see a free river, and keep the pot size manageable.

The board is Tc-6d-5c-9h-3h, and I am holding Ace-high with no pair or straight potential. My opponent called my flop continuation bet, indicating they likely have at least a pair or a strong draw that hit the board. Betting here would be a pure bluff with very low expected success, as my opponent has shown resilience. Checking is the most prudent play to minimize losses, as I am effectively folding to any significant bet and there is no value in betting my hand.

On the river, the 3h is a blank. Flashlight checks for a second time, which strongly suggests he has a missed draw (like clubs or overcards) or a marginal made hand (like A-high, 6x, or 5x) that he is trying to take to showdown. With pocket sevens, we have decent showdown value against his missed draws and unpaired high cards. However, we do not have a strong enough hand to value bet, as worse hands are unlikely to call and better hands (like 9x or Tx) are unlikely to fold. Checking behind is the best play to realize our showdown equity without inflating the pot or risking more chips.

I am holding Ace-King offsuit, which is one of the strongest starting hands in a three-handed game. Opening with a raise is the standard and most profitable play here to establish initiative, apply pressure to the blinds, and build a pot while in position.

I am dealt a very weak offsuit hand (T2o) in the Small Blind. Facing a 3x raise from the Button, there is no strategic value in defending this trash out of position. A fold is the standard and correct play.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Eight offsuit. The button (Flashlight) has raised to 3x the big blind, and the Small Blind has folded. K8o is a strong enough hand to defend my blind against a standard button opening range, especially in a three-handed game where hand values are slightly inflated. The pot odds are favorable (calling 200 to win a total pot of 450), and I will have the advantage of being in position relative to the Small Blind if they had called, though here I am closing the action and will be out of position against the Button post-flop. I will call to see the flop.

I am out of position in a heads-up pot after calling a pre-flop raise from the button. The flop has come down monotone hearts (Jh 6h 7h). I hold King-Eight offsuit with no hearts, meaning I have no flush draw and no pair. Checking is the standard and most prudent play here to see how the pre-flop aggressor reacts to this board texture. Betting into a monotone board without a heart or a strong made hand would be overly risky.

The flop is monotone, containing three hearts, which significantly changes the board texture. Holding Ace-King without a heart leaves me with essentially just high-card strength and no equity against a made flush or even a single heart. Betting into this board on the flop with no heart is risky and prone to getting check-raised by an opponent who may already have a flush or a strong draw. Checking behind allows me to control the pot size, see a free turn card, and re-evaluate if I pick up equity.

The turn brings a fourth heart to the board. I hold no hearts, so I am currently playing the board's flush. Since I am out of position and have no hand strength beyond the community cards, checking is the most logical play. Betting would be a pure bluff and unlikely to succeed given that any single heart in my opponent's hand beats me.

The board texture is extremely dangerous, showing four hearts after the turn card. Since I do not hold a heart in my hand, I have no flush and am highly likely to be behind any opponent who has even a single heart. Betting here would likely induce a check-raise that forces me to fold, or simply get called by a better hand. Checking is the most disciplined and strategic play to control the pot size and take a free look at the river.

The board has five hearts, including the Ace of hearts. This means the board itself is the best possible hand (an Ace-high flush) and all players will split the pot. There is no strategic value in betting, as I cannot have a better hand and my opponent is unlikely to fold their share of a guaranteed split pot. Checking is the most straightforward play.

The board shows four hearts, and I do not hold a heart. My hand of Ace-King with the Ace of Spades is extremely weak in this context, as any single heart in the opponent's range makes a flush. Betting here would be counterproductive, as I would only be called by better hands (any flush) while potentially folding out worse. Checking behind allows me to see the showdown safely without risking further chips on a board where I am effectively playing my high-card strength, which is likely dominated.

I am first to act on the Button with Queen-Four offsuit, which is a very weak starting hand. Given the poor playability and lack of connectivity or suit, the optimal play is to fold and wait for a better spot to steal or play a pot.

I am in the Small Blind with 9d 5c. The Button has folded. While it's only 50 chips to complete the bet to the Big Blind, 95o is a very weak hand with poor playability, especially since I would be out of position for the rest of the hand against Flashlight. Given the hand's lack of strength and my position, folding is the most disciplined play to avoid losing chips in a marginal situation.

I am on the button with Ah 6c. This is a standard opening hand in a three-handed game. My stack is healthy at over 100 big blinds. Raising allows me to take control of the hand and potentially win the blinds immediately or play a pot in position. I will open for a standard 3x raise to 300.

Holding 5c 8d out of position against a button open is a clear fold. The hand has poor equity and playing out of position will lead to difficult post-flop spots. Folding is the disciplined play to preserve stack integrity.
Q3 offsuit is a very weak hand to defend from the big blind against a 3x button open. Folding is the optimal decision here to preserve my stack.

K7o is a weak holding, even for a button open in a 3-handed game. Given the aggression observed from the other players in earlier hands, opening this hand creates unnecessary risk of being 3-bet or forced into a difficult post-flop scenario with a dominated King. Maintaining stack discipline and waiting for a better spot is the optimal strategy.

With King-Three offsuit in the Small Blind and the Button having folded, I have a very weak hand with poor post-flop playability. Although I could attempt a steal, doing so out of position with a marginal holding is risky, especially when the Big Blind can defend a wide range. Folding is the most straightforward and disciplined play here.

I am on the Button in a 3-handed game with 87o. This is a weak, offsuit connector that plays poorly post-flop and lacks sufficient equity to open-raise profitably in this spot. The standard and safest play is to fold and wait for a stronger starting hand.

I am in the Small Blind with Jc 8c, a decent suited semi-connector. The Button (Pro) has folded, so I am heads-up against the Big Blind (Flashlight). Since I only need to call 50 more to see a flop, and I have a hand that plays well post-flop, calling is a standard and safe play to realize my equity. However, I can also raise to put pressure on the Big Blind, but since Flashlight has been active, I will simply complete the small blind to see a cheap flop and minimize risk with a mid-strength hand.

The small blind has completed the blind, and I am in the big blind with a weak, offsuit hand (4sTs). Since I have already posted the big blind, I can check and see the flop for free without committing extra chips. There is no value in raising here with this holding.

In this limped pot, the flop of Kc 6s 2s is relatively dry. I have Jack-high with no direct draws. Since I am out of position against the Big Blind and have very little equity in this hand currently, checking is the most prudent play to see how the opponent reacts. Betting into a limped pot with no pair and no draw is unnecessary risk.

The flop comes Kc 6s 2s. I have 4sTs, providing a backdoor flush draw, but no made hand. Flash checked from the small blind. Checking behind is the standard play here as it allows me to realize my equity and see the turn for free, while keeping the pot manageable.

On the turn, I have improved to second pair with a Jack. Given that the flop was checked through, it is unlikely that Flashlight has a King. I will bet 100, which is half the pot, to extract value from weaker pairs (6s or 2s) and to charge any spade flush draws that may have picked up equity on the turn.

The opponent (Flash) bet 100 into a 200 pot on the turn, which is a small bet. I have the Ten of Spades, giving me a flush draw with the cards on the board (6s, 2s, Js). Given the favorable pot odds (3:1) and the potential implied odds if I hit my flush on the river, calling is the mathematically correct and standard play to realize my equity.

On the river, the board is Kc 6s 2s Js Th. I have a pair of Jacks. The turn bet was called by Flashlight, suggesting they have some piece of the board or a draw. The Ten on the river completes some straight possibilities (AQ, Q9) but misses the spade flush draw. Given that Flashlight checked the flop and only called the turn, it is unlikely they have a King. However, my Jack-Eight is a medium-strength hand that has good showdown value. Betting might only get called by hands that beat me (like a King, Two-Pair, or a Straight), while checking allows me to see a showdown for free or potentially induce a bluff from missed spade draws. I will check to realize my equity.

I hold a pair of Tens (Ts paired with the board Th). The board texture is K-J-T-6-2, which is coordinated and dangerous. Since Flash checked to me, I have showdown value, but my hand is essentially a bluff catcher. Betting would not likely extract value from worse hands and would only commit chips against better hands that might check-raise. Therefore, I will check behind to take my marginal hand to showdown.

I am on the button with 7-5 offsuit. While 3-handed play requires a wider opening range, 7-5 offsuit is generally too weak to justify an open-raise, especially considering I have been relatively active in recent hands. I will fold and wait for a more profitable spot.

Holding 7-3 offsuit is a very weak hand with poor potential for post-flop equity. Playing out of position from the small blind with such a trash hand is not a profitable strategy. Folding is the most disciplined and effective choice to preserve my stack.

The starting hand of King-Three offsuit is weak, lacking sufficient connection or high-card strength to justify a call in a three-handed game. As I am in the button position and facing a requirement of 100 chips to enter the pot, the most disciplined and professional action is to fold and preserve my stack for a better hand.
With AQo in the small blind and it folded to me, I have a premium hand. I want to raise to build the pot, take the initiative, or just win the blinds outright. A 3x raise to 300 is standard.

I am in the Big Blind holding King-Three offsuit (K3o). The Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 300, which is a common blind-vs-blind aggression. While K3o is not a premium hand, it has some high-card value. However, being out of position against a competent opponent who showed aggression pre-flop makes calling or raising risky. Given the early stage of the tournament (Hand 19 of 150) and my healthy stack, I will fold this marginal hand to avoid unnecessary post-flop complications without a strong starting hand or position.
A3s is a standard open from the button. Raising to 2.5x (250) applies pressure to the blinds and allows us to play in position with a hand that has good playability and nut-making potential.

I am in the Small Blind with 8c 9c, which is a strong suited connector. The Button (Pro) has raised to 250. While playing out of position is not ideal, 8c 9c has excellent playability and the pot odds (calling 200 to win a pot of 400) make this a standard call to see a flop. I will call and see how the board develops.

With 5s 4s in the big blind, I am getting 4:1 pot odds (150 to call to win a 600 pot). Suited connectors play well in multi-way pots and provide strong implied odds if I hit a straight or flush draw. Folding is too conservative given the favorable price, and raising here would be an over-bluff in a multi-way scenario. Calling is the mathematically sound play.

I have 9-high on a board with a pair of Kings and a Jack. This board heavily favors the pre-flop raiser's range and offers me no significant draws. Checking is the most prudent play here to see how the action unfolds and avoid bloating a pot with no equity.

The flop of Js-Kh-Kc is highly disconnected from my hand of 5s-4s. I have no pair, no straight draws, and no flush draw. Since both opponents have checked, I have the opportunity to see a free turn card. I will check, but I am prepared to fold to any meaningful bet from the button or small blind, as the board heavily favors the pre-flop aggressor's range.

With Ace-high and a backdoor club draw on a paired King board multi-way, checking back is a safe and standard play. The blinds' calling ranges pre-flop contain a lot of broadway hands that could easily have connected with a King or a Jack. By checking, I control the size of the pot, keep my marginal showdown value, and get a free card to potentially improve on the turn.

On the turn, the 5 of clubs gives me a backdoor flush draw, but I still only have 9-high. Everyone checked the flop, which suggests the King or Jack might not be out there, or the pre-flop raiser is playing cautiously. However, since I am out of position against two opponents and have no made hand, checking again is the most disciplined play to see a free river or evaluate based on my opponents' actions.

The board texture is highly favorable for the pre-flop aggressor (Pro), who is very likely to have a King or a Jack in their range. My pair of fives is extremely weak and unlikely to win at showdown if there is significant betting. Betting here would only isolate me against better hands, as I would get called by any pair better than fives. Checking allows me to control the pot size and potentially see a free river card, which is the most prudent strategy given my relative hand strength.

With the turn bringing a second club, I've picked up the nut flush draw to go with my Ace overcard. Both opponents have checked to me on the flop and turn, showing weakness and likely indicating they don't hold a King. This is a great spot to fire a semi-bluff bet to try and take down the pot right now against weak pairs or better Ace-highs, while still having strong equity if called.

I am currently holding 9-high with a backdoor flush draw on a board of Js Kh Kc 5c. Pro, the pre-flop aggressor, has bet 350 into a 750 pot after everyone checked the flop. While the pot odds are roughly 4 to 1 and I have a flush draw (approximately 19% chance to hit on the river), there are several concerns. First, Flashlight is still to act behind me and could raise. Second, even if I hit my flush, the paired Kings on the board mean I could be drawing dead to a full house or a higher flush. Given the board texture and my lack of showdown value, calling here is too risky. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

The board is paired with Kings, making it very likely that the pre-flop aggressor (Pro) has a strong hand like a King or at least a Jack. My pair of fives is extremely weak here. Even if Pro is semi-bluffing with a draw, I am out of position and would face a tough decision on the river regardless. Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with KTo (King of Spades, Ten of Clubs). In a three-handed game, this is a strong starting hand and a standard opening candidate. I will raise to 3x the big blind (300 chips) to take control of the pot and apply pressure on the blinds.

Flash is on the button and has been opening frequently. My hand, Queen-Nine offsuit, is a playable holding in the small blind, though somewhat speculative. With a stack of 9125 and a raise to 300, the pot odds are decent to justify a call, especially since I can see the flop in position relative to the button if the big blind folds, or play a three-way pot. Folding would be too tight given the button's aggressive opening range.

In a 3-handed game, ranges are significantly wider. A9o is a reasonably strong hand against a Button open and a Small Blind call. While the pot odds are roughly 4.6:1, calling with Ace-high out of position on such a connected board is speculative and likely to lead to further difficult decisions on the turn. Without a clear path to improvement or a profitable bluffing opportunity, folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my chip lead.

I am dealt 7c 4h on the Button, which is an extremely weak, unsuited starting hand. In a 3-handed game, acting first pre-flop, there is no reason to invest chips with trash hands. The most optimal and standard play is to fold and wait for better cards.

I am in the Small Blind with 8d 9s. It has folded to me. While 89o is a marginal hand, the cost to see a flop is only 50 more chips into a pot of 150. Given my chip lead, I can afford to complete the blind and see a flop, although I will be out of position for the rest of the hand. Raising is also an option, but Flashlight (BB) has shown some aggression recently, so keeping the pot small with a medium-strength drawing hand is a safer way to realize equity.

I am in the Big Blind with a very weak hand (3c, 7d). The Small Blind (Flash) has simply completed the blind. There is no reason to inflate the pot with such a poor holding. I will check to see a flop for free.

On a board of Jh-Kh-6s, my 9-high (8d 9s) has almost no equity. I have no pair and no straight or flush draws. Since I am out of position against the Big Blind in a small limped pot, the most prudent play is to check. I am prepared to fold if there is any significant betting action, as this board likely connects better with a random Big Blind range than my own hand.

I am in the Big Blind with a very weak hand (7-high) on a board of Jh-Kh-6s. I have no pair, no draw, and essentially zero equity. The opponent checked to me, which is common on a board that hits a calling/completing range reasonably well. There is no benefit to betting here as I would be purely bluffing with no fold equity and no plan for the turn. Checking is the only logical action to maintain a low pot and re-evaluate on the turn or fold to any significant aggression.

The Ace of Diamonds on the turn makes the board even more dangerous, as it now contains an Ace, King, and Jack. Since I have only 9-high with no draws, there is no value in betting. Flashlight checked back the flop, but could easily have a piece of this board or a drawing hand. I will check and fold to any bet, as I have zero equity in this pot.

My hand is 3c-7d, which has absolutely no showdown value or draw potential on a board of Jh-Kh-6s-Ad. The opponent (Small Blind) has checked to me. Betting into them with seven-high is a low-percentage bluff that is unlikely to succeed against a range that includes many pairs or strong high cards that hit this board. Checking is the only prudent decision to reach the river without committing more chips to a lost cause.

I have hit a pair of eights on the river. Given the board contains an Ace, King, and Jack, my pair is very weak. However, since the Big Blind has checked through the flop and turn, it is unlikely they have a very strong hand. I will check to see if I can get to a cheap showdown. If the opponent bets, I will evaluate the size, but checking is the most prudent play here to minimize risk in a small pot.

I have 3-high on a board of Jh-Kh-6s-Ad-8c. This is essentially the lowest possible hand, and I have absolutely no showdown value. The opponent has checked, so I am not facing a bet. There is no incentive to bluff here because my hand has zero equity, and any bet is highly likely to be called by any pair or even better high-card combinations that the opponent might have checked down. Checking is the only logical action to conclude the hand without wasting more chips.

I am on the button with 63o, which is a very weak hand. Given the stack sizes and the tendency of both Flashlight and Pro to defend their blinds or 3-bet, there is no value in attempting a steal with this bottom-tier hand. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the small blind with a weak hand (4c-8s). Since the button has folded, I am facing the big blind. Playing 4-8 offsuit out of position against the big blind is not profitable in the long run. I will fold to minimize losses.

I am on the button with 7-3 suited, which is a very weak hand in a 3-handed game. There is no incentive to play this hand aggressively or to limp into a pot against two opponents. Folding is the most disciplined and statistically sound play.

With Q9o in the small blind after the button folds, I have a reasonable hand to attack the big blind. A raise to 3bb (300 chips) puts pressure on the BB, allows me to take the initiative, and frequently wins the pot outright pre-flop.

I am in the Big Blind with J8o. The Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 300. While I have position post-flop, J8o is a relatively weak hand to defend against a raise, even from the Small Blind. Given the current stack sizes and my position as the chip leader, I prefer to fold and wait for a stronger starting hand or a more favorable situation rather than getting involved in a marginal spot out of position or with a mediocre hand.

4h Th is a very weak suited hand (a six-gapper). Even from the button, it is not strong enough to open-raise profitably because it lacks high card strength and playability post-flop. The standard optimal play is to fold.

I am in the Small Blind with K2o. The Button has folded, leaving only me and the Big Blind. While K2o is a relatively weak hand, in a Blind vs. Blind situation, it is often strong enough to open for a raise to try and take down the blinds or play a heads-up pot with the initiative. Flashlight (BB) has shown some aggression in previous hands by 3-betting, so if I face a significant re-raise, I will likely fold, but I will start by raising to 300.

I am in the Big Blind with 6d 9s, a very weak offsuit hand. Flash has raised to 300, and I need to call 200 more to see a flop. While the pot odds are tempting, playing 6-9 offsuit out of position is a losing strategy in the long run. I should fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with Q3 offsuit. While a wide opening range is standard in 3-handed play, Q3o is at the very bottom of the range and generally too weak to open against competent opponents who may defend their blinds or 3-bet. With my current chip lead, I can afford to wait for better spots rather than forcing a steal with a marginal hand.

I am in the small blind with a very weak hand (3-5 offsuit). Playing this hand out of position against the big blind is negative expected value. Folding is the disciplined choice to preserve my stack.

Ten-deuce suited is a weak hand even in a three-handed game. Playing from the button with such a marginal holding offers poor equity realization, and it is better to conserve my stack and wait for a stronger starting hand to enter the pot.
J4o is a very weak, uncoordinated hand. Being out of position in the small blind against the big blind, the most profitable and standard play is to fold.

Q8 offsuit is a weak hand to open, even from the button. While stealing the blinds can sometimes be profitable if opponents overfold, this hand lacks the playability to continue if called or 3-bet. Folding is the standard and safest play here to preserve chips for better opportunities.

I am in the Small Blind with a strong starting hand (Ah Jd) after the Button has folded. Against only the Big Blind remaining, this is a clear raising situation to either win the pot immediately or play a heads-up pot with a range advantage. A standard 3x open is appropriate here.

I hold 3c-7s, which is a very weak hand with almost no potential to make a strong range. Calling 200 into a pot of 400 while out of position against an aggressive player like Flash is a negative expected value (-EV) play. It is disciplined to fold here and preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am on the button with Jack-Eight offsuit. While it is a three-handed game and aggression from the button is generally rewarded, J8o is a marginal hand that often underperforms against the calling or three-betting ranges of the blinds, especially considering Flashlight has shown a willingness to three-bet recently. With a healthy chip lead, I choose to fold this hand and wait for a better opportunity to exert pressure.

Ac-Tc suited is a premium hand in a blind-versus-blind confrontation. Raising to 300 chips (3x the big blind) is the optimal strategic move to either claim the pot immediately or isolate the Big Blind in a favorable position with a strong range.

Flashlight opened to 3 big blinds from the Small Blind. I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Jack offsuit. This hand has good equity against a wide SB opening range and plays very well in position post-flop. Calling is the standard and most profitable line here to keep his weaker bluffs in the pot and evaluate on the flop.

The flop is highly coordinated (7s 6h 8c), which hits a Big Blind's calling range quite well. However, as the pre-flop aggressor, I should maintain initiative. My holding of Ac-Tc gives me backdoor flush equity and a gutshot straight draw (requiring a 9). A continuation bet of 350 (slightly over half-pot) is appropriate to apply pressure on marginal holdings and gain information about the opponent's strength on this dangerous board.

I missed the flop completely on a highly connected 7-6-8 board, which hits my pre-flop calling range much better than the Small Blind's opening range, but unfortunately my specific hand (QJo) missed entirely. With just queen-high, no direct straight draw, and no flush draw, calling or raising a continuation bet of more than half pot is unprofitable. The best play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

I am dealt 2h 3h on the button, which is one of the weakest possible starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Despite having positional advantage, there is no strategic value in playing this hand against two opponents. The cost of calling the big blind is not justified by the potential equity of this holding. Therefore, folding is the most disciplined and correct decision to preserve my stack.

A7o is a solid hand to steal from the small blind when the action folds to me. Raising to 3x (300) puts pressure on the big blind and allows me to take down the pot pre-flop or enter the flop with the betting lead and range advantage.

I am in the Big Blind with 8s 7d. The Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 300. I need to call 200 more into a pot of 400, giving me 3:1 pot odds. While 87o is a marginal hand and I will be out of position, the price is good enough to defend my blind and see a flop, especially with deep stacks of over 90 big blinds.

I completely missed the Q-10-4 rainbow flop with A7o. This board heavily favors the Big Blind's calling range, which contains many broadway combinations, Qx, and Tx hands. With only Ace-high and no backdoor draws, a continuation bet is unlikely to succeed often enough to be profitable. Checking to pot control or prepare for a fold is the safest and most optimal play here.

I am in position against the Small Blind in a heads-up pot. The flop of Qs-4h-Td is quite dry and favors the pre-flop raiser's range. However, Pro has checked to me. While I could attempt a bluff to take the pot down now, my hand (8-high) has almost no equity if called. Checking back allows me to see a free turn card and potentially improve or evaluate Pro's action on the next street without inflating the pot with a weak hand.

The turn card is a Jack, completing a very coordinated board of Q-4-10-J. This texture heavily favors the Big Blind's pre-flop calling range, which contains many broadway cards, two pairs, and straights (like K9, 98, or any Kx). While my hand has picked up a gutshot straight draw to the nut straight (if a King falls), a bluff here is unlikely to succeed given how well this board connects with my opponent. Checking is the safest option to realize my equity and pot control.

I am in position with 8-high on a board of Qs-4h-Td-Jc. The pre-flop raiser (Pro) has checked twice. While this board is very coordinated and likely hits many hands in a pre-flop raising range, Pro's lack of aggression suggests they may have missed or are playing a marginal hand cautiously. However, my hand has virtually no showdown value and very little equity to improve. Checking back is the most prudent play to see a free river card; if I improve to a straight with a 9, I might have a value-betting opportunity, otherwise I can safely fold to any river aggression.

On the river, the 5 of clubs doesn't change much. The board is still heavily coordinated and hits my opponent's pre-flop calling range, which contains many one-pair hands like small pocket pairs, or weak Tx and Jx that decided to check down. Since my Ace-high has decent showdown value against missed draws (like K9, 98, or smaller Ace-highs), checking is the optimal play. A bluff here would struggle to fold out any pair, and a value bet would never get called by a worse hand. Checking allows me to realize my showdown equity without risking more chips.

I am in position with 8-high on a board of Qs-4h-Td-Jc-5c. The pre-flop raiser (Pro) has checked all three streets (Flop, Turn, and River). While my 8-high has no showdown value and will almost certainly lose if I check, my previous checks on the flop and turn make a river bluff look unconvincing. Pro likely has some showdown value like Ace-high or a small pair that they are content to check down. Since the pot is relatively small and I have no real equity, I will check behind and concede the hand rather than risking chips on a low-frequency bluff.
J7s is a marginal but playable hand from the Button. Given the fold equity against the blinds and positional advantage post-flop, raising to 2.5x (250) is a profitable steal attempt.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Seven suited. The Button (Pro) has opened for a 2.5x raise (250). While playing out of position is not ideal, T7s has reasonable playability and equity in a three-handed game. Given that I have the largest stack and am getting 2:1 odds on the call (200 to win a pot of 400), I will call to see a flop and evaluate. This is consistent with my previous play where I have defended the blinds with similar or slightly weaker hands like 87o.

I am in the Big Blind with a very weak holding, Jack-three offsuit. While the pot odds (150 to call into a pot of 600) are tempting, my hand has very little equity and I will be out of position for the remainder of the hand against two opponents who have shown aggression. Continuing with this hand is a losing proposition, so I will fold.

On the flop of Qc-4d-8s, I have Ten-high with a backdoor straight draw and a backdoor flush draw. The board is relatively dry, and I am the pre-flop caller out of position against the original raiser (Pro). Since I checked and there is no bet yet, I will check to see how the action develops and whether I can realize my equity or take the pot later. Checking is the standard play for the caller out of position on this type of board texture.

With Jack-high and only weak backdoor draws, I have very little equity on this Qc-4d-8s board. However, this dry board texture heavily favors my pre-flop button raising range, as I can have many strong Queens (AQ, KQ, QJ) and overpairs (AA, KK). The Small Blind's flat-calling range contains many missed broadways (like AK, AJ, KJ), suited connectors, and small pairs that will fold to a continuation bet. A small c-bet of 1/3 pot is a cheap and effective way to capitalize on fold equity and take down the pot immediately.

I am facing a 1/3 pot continuation bet (200 into 600) on a dry board of Qc-4d-8s. My hand, Ten-Seven of spades, has completely missed this flop, providing only a backdoor flush draw and very weak backdoor straight possibilities. While the price to call is relatively small (200 to win a total pot of 1000), I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor with only Ten-high. Without a pair or a strong draw, calling here would essentially be a speculative play hoping to hit a specific turn card or bluff later. Given the tournament context and my stack size, I prefer to fold and wait for a more advantageous situation rather than peeling with minimal equity.

I am on the button with King-Eight offsuit in a three-handed game. This is a standard opening hand from the button. I have been consistently opening for 300 (3x BB) throughout the tournament, and I will maintain that sizing here to put pressure on the blinds and potentially take the pot down pre-flop or play a pot in position.

I am in the small blind with a very weak hand (2c 4d). Against a raise from the button, there is no strategic justification for calling or defending. The best play is to fold and preserve my stack for a more favorable situation.

I am in the Big Blind with QJo facing a 3bb raise from the Button. QJo is a solid broadway hand that has plenty of equity against a wide Button opening range. It is generally too strong to fold but plays better as a call rather than a 3-bet, allowing me to defend the blind and evaluate the flop.

I am first to act on an Ah 6c Jd flop after defending the Big Blind. I have middle pair with a Queen kicker. On an Ace-high board, the pre-flop raiser (Flash) has a significant range and nut advantage. Leading out (donk betting) here makes little sense, as it would likely only be called by better hands like Aces, better Jacks, or strong draws. The standard and most sound play is to check to the pre-flop raiser to evaluate their action.

On a board of Ah-6c-Jd, I have King-high with an overcard to the Jack, but I missed the flop completely. The Big Blind (Pro) checked, which is standard. This Ace-high board favors my perceived range as the pre-flop raiser from the button. I will place a continuation bet of 325, which is half the pot, to represent an Ace or a strong Jack and force folds from hands like medium pairs or weaker high-card hands that might have floated pre-flop.

Facing a half-pot continuation bet from the Button on a dry Ah 6c Jd rainbow board, our Jc Qh gives us middle pair with a strong kicker. While Flash's range includes many Aces that connect with this board, it also contains numerous bluffs, missed broadway cards (like KQ, KT, QT), and lower pocket pairs that are c-betting. Folding second pair to a single bet here would be over-folding against an aggressive Button range. Calling is the standard play to defend our Big Blind, keeping his bluffs in, with the plan to re-evaluate on the turn.

On a relatively blank turn card (2h), the board texture hasn't fundamentally changed, though a backdoor heart draw has appeared. As the pre-flop caller out of position, leading into the aggressor (donk betting) is generally a poor strategy because it folds out their bluffs while isolating us against their strong value hands (like Ax). The standard and optimal play is to check to the Button, keeping their range wide and allowing them to potentially fire another bluff or check back.

The turn card is a deuce of hearts, which is largely a blank. My opponent called my flop continuation bet on an Ace-high board, suggesting they likely have a piece of it, such as an Ace, a Jack, or perhaps a straight draw like QT. Since my hand has no equity and very little showdown value, and a second barrel is unlikely to fold out an Ace, checking back is the best move to control the pot size and see a free river card.

On the river, the board pairs the 6 and brings in a backdoor heart flush. Flash checked back the turn, which usually caps their range at a marginal Ace, a Jack, or a lower pocket pair, along with some missed draws. With Queen-Jack, we have a decent bluff-catcher (Jacks and Sixes). However, betting for value here is too thin because worse hands (like 88-TT or missed broadway cards) will likely fold, while better hands (like any Ace or a flush) will call or raise. Checking allows us to get to showdown cheaply and gives Flash an opportunity to bluff with their missed draws.

On this board, my King-high has very little showdown value, but I have effectively represented a capped range by checking the turn. My opponent's call on the flop suggests they have a piece of the board, likely an Ace, a Jack, or a six. Since Pro checked the river, they are either trapping with a marginal made hand or giving up with a draw. A bluff is unlikely to fold out an Ace or a Jack given the pot odds and my previous check. Therefore, I will check behind to see the showdown without risking further chips.

I am on the button with 9c 8c in a 3-handed game. This is a strong position to open the pot with suited connectors, allowing me to potentially take the blinds or play a hand in position. A raise to 300 is a standard sizing to build the pot and maintain the initiative.
Q8o is a weak offsuit hand, especially out of position against a button raise. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here to preserve chips.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Jack offsuit, which is a very strong hand in 3-handed play. The button (Flashlight) raised to 300, which is a standard opening range. Since I am out of position for the rest of the hand, I want to 3-bet to take the initiative and potentially end the hand immediately. AJo is well ahead of a typical button opening range, and raising to 1000 puts pressure on the shorter stack and builds a pot while I likely have the best hand.

I am facing a 3-bet from the Big Blind after my open. While 9c 8c is a speculative hand, I have position and the pot odds are reasonable enough to justify a call to see a flop. The 3-bet sizing isn't overwhelming relative to our stack depths, so I will call and look to realize my equity in position.

I 3-bet pre-flop with Ace-Jack offsuit and was called by the button. The flop comes Kd-Qd-7s, which is quite coordinated and hits a lot of the button's calling range (Kx, Qx, and straight draws like JT). However, as the pre-flop aggressor, I still have a range advantage. Checking here is a standard play to see how the opponent reacts, as betting into two overcards where I only have Ace-high can be risky. If the opponent checks back, I can re-evaluate on the turn. If they bet, I may have to fold unless I pick up a draw, but given the pot size and the board, a check is the most prudent starting move to control the pot.

I am holding 9c 8c on a board of Qd Kc 7s. I called a 3-bet pre-flop, so the pot is bloated, but I have essentially no equity here—no pair, no strong draw, just high-card air. The opponent (Big Blind) checked to me, which is often a trap or a check-give-up. Given that this board texture heavily favors the 3-betting range, betting into them with nothing is high risk and low reward. Checking back allows me to see a turn card for free and keeps the pot manageable, which is the most disciplined approach with this weak holding.

The turn 8d provides me with the nut flush draw to go along with my gutshot straight draw (needing a Ten). Since the flop went check-check, it's unlikely Flashlight has a very strong hand like top pair or better, as those would typically bet for protection on a coordinated board. By checking again, I am prioritizing realizing my equity. If Flashlight bets, I have an easy call given my high number of outs (9 diamonds and 3 non-diamond Tens). If it goes check-check, I get a free card to potentially hit the nuts on the river.

I have paired my 8 on the turn, giving me a pair of 8s on a board of Qd-Kc-7s-8d. While this is a marginal hand, it has some showdown value. The opponent checked the flop and the turn, which likely signals weakness or a trap. Betting here is risky as it would force the opponent to fold their complete air (which I currently beat) or call/raise with better hands (like a King or Queen). By checking back, I maintain pot control, realize my showdown equity, and see the river for free.

I have improved to a pair of Jacks on the river, but the board is extremely coordinated (Q-K-7-8-J), meaning any Ten makes a straight. The action has been passive since my pre-flop 3-bet, with both of us checking the flop and turn. This suggests Flashlight might have a marginal hand or a draw. By checking the river, I am looking to reach a cheap showdown. I likely have the best hand against missed draws or lower pairs, but I am behind any King, Queen, or straight. Betting would likely only get called by hands that beat me.

The board texture is Q-K-7-8-J, which is highly connected and includes several overcards to my pair of 8s. Any hand containing a 10 has a straight, and any hand with a higher card (A, K, Q, J) beats me. The opponent has checked the flop, turn, and river, suggesting they have a weak holding, but betting my 8-pair into this board is unlikely to get called by worse while being easily beaten by anything better. Checking back is the most disciplined way to reach showdown and protect my stack.
I'm holding 72 offsuit on the button, which is the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em. There is no reason to put any chips into the pot with this hand. It's an easy fold.

I am in the Small Blind with Qc 4c. The button (Pro) has folded, leaving only me and the Big Blind (Flashlight) in the hand. Qc 4c is a reasonable hand to attempt a steal with, especially since I've been maintaining an aggressive image by opening for 3x (300) in many previous hands. Flashlight has a shorter stack of 6525 (65 BBs), which might make him more cautious about defending with marginal hands. I will raise to 300 to try and take the pot down immediately or take control of the hand going into the flop.

I am in the Big Blind with Jd 6c, which is a weak hand. Flash has raised to 300 from the Small Blind. Given the poor quality of my hand and the lack of incentive to defend it against a raise, folding is the most disciplined and prudent action to preserve my stack.

As the chip leader on the button in a three-handed game, holding Ace-Trey offsuit is a standard opening hand to put pressure on the blinds. A raise to 300 is consistent with the established betting patterns of the table and allows me to play in position if called.

J-10 offsuit is a marginal hand that has decent equity against an aggressive button's wide opening range. While playing out of position can be difficult, the pot odds (needing to call 250 into a 450 pot) justify a call here to see a flop.

I am in the Big Blind holding 3s 4c, which is a very weak and unsuited starting hand. The Button has opened for 3 big blinds and the Small Blind has called. Despite getting favorable pot odds (200 to call into a pot of 700), my hand has terrible equity against two opponents and very poor playability post-flop. Playing out of position with such marginal cards is a losing play in the long run. Folding is the best decision.

I have middle pair (tens) on a relatively dry board (2h-Th-6s). Checking is the standard play when out of position against the button pre-flop aggressor. This allows me to control the pot size and evaluate Flash's continuation bet before committing more chips.

I am the pre-flop aggressor and have been checked to on a relatively dry Ten-high board. While Ace-high has some showdown value, a continuation bet is standard here to put pressure on the Small Blind's calling range, which likely consists of medium pocket pairs, suited connectors, or Broadway cards that may have missed this flop. A bet of 350 (half pot) is sufficient to fold out many hands with equity against me while keeping the pot manageable if I am called.

I have middle pair with a decent kicker on a board that is relatively dry, although it does have two hearts. I hold the Jack of hearts, giving me some backdoor flush equity. Flash (the button) often bets this type of board with overcards or air to take down the pot. With the pot offering 3:1 odds (350 to call into a 1050 pot), calling is mathematically sound and avoids being forced off my equity too easily. I will call the bet and re-evaluate on the turn.

I am in the Small Blind against the Button (Flash). The board is 2h-Th-6s-5h. I hold Jh-Td, giving me a pair of tens and a flush draw with the Jack of hearts. Given I am out of position and Flash was the aggressor on the flop, checking is the most standard and defensive play. It allows me to evaluate Flash's reaction to the flush draw hitting the board and potentially check-call a reasonable bet or check-raise if I believe I have the best hand, while keeping the pot manageable.

On this turn, the 5h brings a potential flush and completes some straight draws. Flashlight has checked twice, indicating a willingness to see the river or a potential trap. My Ace-high has limited showdown value but is not strong enough to bet for value. Betting here might force out some worse hands but will likely be called by any Ten or better, and a check-raise would be difficult to handle. By checking back, I control the pot size and get a free look at the river, where I might improve to top pair or can evaluate Flashlight's final action.

The river is the 5c, which pairs the board. I hold a pair of tens with a jack kicker. Given the board texture (2h-Th-6s-5h-5c), my tens have some showdown value but are unlikely to be the best hand if Flash has any piece of the board or a higher pair. Since there are no more cards to come, I have no reason to bet for value as worse hands likely won't call, and better hands will call or raise. Checking is the standard play to reach showdown as cheaply as possible and avoid bloating the pot against a wider range.

Flashlight has checked for the third consecutive time since calling my flop bet. This line typically suggests a hand with some showdown value that is being played cautiously (like a weak Ten or a middle pair) or a missed draw. While Ace-high has some showdown value against missed overcards like KQ or KJ, it is unlikely to beat any pair. However, betting here would turn my hand into a bluff, and it's unclear if Flashlight would fold a weak Ten or a mid-pair after investing in the pot on the flop. Given the board pairing on the river and the flush completing on the turn, checking back to see the showdown is the most conservative and potentially high-EV play to minimize losses or win against pure air.

Being on the button in a three-handed game is a significant positional advantage. Q4s is a playable hand in late position, suitable for an opening raise to attempt to steal the blinds. My sizing of 300 is consistent with my typical opening range and puts pressure on the small and big blinds.
94s is a very weak hand, well outside any standard defending range from the small blind against a button open. I am out of position and the hand has poor playability, so folding is the best option.

I am in the big blind with 3c 7d, which is a very weak hand. Flashlight has raised to 300 from the button, and the small blind has folded. While it's only 200 more to call, 3-7 offsuit plays poorly post-flop and I would be out of position. There is no strategic reason to defend such a weak range against a standard button open in this situation.

Eight-five suited is a weak two-gapper. Although we have the advantage of being on the button, this hand is generally too weak to open-raise for a steal, even in a 3-handed game. It's better to fold and wait for a more playable hand or a stronger spot to get involved.

I am in the Small Blind with QJ-offsuit after the Button has folded. This is a standard opening situation in a three-handed game. My hand is well above the average range for this position. I will raise to 300, which has been my consistent opening size in previous hands, to put pressure on the Big Blind and potentially win the pot immediately or play with the initiative.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Six offsuit, facing a raise from the Small Blind. Flash has been playing very aggressively with a wide range, so I shouldn't give him too much credit for a premium hand. Raising to 300 is a standard move to attempt to take down the blinds and ante dead money immediately, while also setting me up to play a post-flop hand with initiative if I am called.

I am in the small blind with a very weak offsuit hand (Q3o) facing a button raise from Flashlight. This is an easy fold as playing this hand out of position will lead to losing chips over the long run.

I am in the Big Blind with Jd 5h. Flashlight, the button, has raised to 300. The Small Blind (Pro) has folded. I need to call 200 more into a pot that will be 650.
