Flashlight has a relatively short stack compared to me and Pro. Given the hand strength and the disadvantage of being out of position, the best play is to fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my chip lead.

I am on the Button with Jack-Four offsuit, which is a very weak starting hand. Even though I have position, this hand lacks the playability and equity to justify an open-raise or a call. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here.

Folded to me in the Small Blind, King-Seven offsuit is a strong enough hand to raise for value and to attempt to win the pot immediately. I'll make a standard raise to 300 total, which has been my consistent sizing in previous hands. This puts pressure on Flashlight, who has a shorter stack of 4625.

T3o is a very weak hand with poor equity, and calling a raise out of position against Flash is negative expected value. Given the stack sizes and the hand's weakness, folding is the most disciplined and optimal play to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with 8s6c. This is a marginal hand that doesn't play particularly well against a 3-bet or heavy resistance. However, the stack sizes show I am the chip leader, and both opponents have been relatively fold-prone to button steals or have played cautiously post-flop. A standard 3x raise is my usual opening sizing to apply pressure on the blinds. If I am 3-bet, I can easily fold this hand, but given the table dynamics, a raise here often picks up the blinds or allows me to take control of the pot positionally.

KTo is a reasonably strong hand in a three-handed game, particularly when facing a button raise. Being in the small blind puts me out of position, so calling is a more controlled approach than 3-betting, which could lead to an uncomfortable 4-bet situation. I will call the 300 to see a flop and evaluate based on how it hits.

With 8d 4d in the Big Blind, we have a very weak suited hand. Even though we are getting decent pot odds (200 to win 700), playing an 8-high 4-gapper out of position in a multiway pot is a mathematically losing long-term play. Our hand lacks playability and showdown value, so folding is the optimal decision.

I have flopped top pair with a King, which is a strong hand in a heads-up pot. However, I am playing out of position against the button. The board texture is relatively dry, although a heart flush draw is possible. Checking is the most standard play here; it allows me to control the pot size and act as a trap if my opponent has a weaker hand that they might bluff with, or to check-call if they decide to bet for value. I prefer keeping the pot manageable rather than leading out and inflating the pot against a wider range.

I have an open-ended straight draw possibilities (though very weak) and no current pair on a board that contains a King and a Ten. Flashlight checked to me, the pre-flop aggressor. While I don't have a piece of this board, it is a decent board for my perceived range. However, given my specific cards (8 high) and the fact that Flashlight called a raise from the blinds, they may have a piece of this board or a draw themselves. A continuation bet here might take it down, but I'll check to see a free turn card and evaluate if I can improve or represent a hand later.

I have flopped and turned top two pair (Kings and Tens) on a board of Kd, Th, 2h, 9s. My opponent, Flash (the button), checked the flop, which suggests they likely do not have a set or a strong King. Betting here allows me to extract value from weaker pairs, straight draws (like QJ), or flush draws that the opponent may be holding. A bet of 400 in a 700-chip pot is an appropriate size to build value while still keeping the opponent's range wide enough to call.

On the turn, the board is King-Ten-Nine-Deuce. I currently hold 8-high and have a gutshot straight draw needing a Seven. Flashlight has led into me for 400 after I checked back the flop. Given the board texture, it is highly likely Flashlight has a piece of the board (a King, Ten, or Nine) or a superior draw like Jack-Queen or Jack-Eight. Calling 400 to win 1100 with only a weak gutshot and no showdown value is not profitable, especially since even hitting a Seven might not result in the best hand if an opponent has a higher straight. I will fold and maintain my stack lead.

Pocket Tens are a premium pair, especially in a three-handed game. Being on the button, I have a positional advantage. A standard raise of 300 is appropriate to build the pot and potentially isolate one of the blinds.
T8 offsuit is too weak of a hand to play from the small blind against a button open. The most profitable decision here is to simply fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Big Blind with Jh5h. The button (Flashlight) raised to 300. I need to call 200 more into a pot of 450. Jh5h is a suited hand that has decent equity and playability in a 3-handed game, especially getting favorable pot odds to defend. Flashlight has a shorter stack (~47 BBs), which makes me cautious about 3-betting light as they might jam, but calling to see a flop and potentially outplaying them post-flop or hitting a strong hand is a solid high-roller strategy.

On this flop of As Kd Qs, I have a gutshot straight draw with my Jack of hearts. However, this board is extremely favorable for the pre-flop raiser's range (Flashlight), as they will have many Ace-x, King-x, Queen-x, and broadway combos. While I have a straight draw, I am out of position and the board is very heavy with high cards and two spades. Since I am first to act and checking is standard on such a coordinated board against the aggressor, I will check and see how Flashlight reacts.

The flop is extremely dangerous with Ace-King-Queen on the board. My pocket tens are essentially a gutshot straight draw to a Jack and have very little showdown value against a calling range that likely contains many broadway combinations. Betting into this wet board with a whiffed hand is ill-advised as it will likely get called or raised by any pair or better. Checking behind is the most prudent play to maintain pot control and realize equity if a Jack appears on the turn.

On the turn 5c, I have improved to bottom pair (5h). The board is extremely high-card heavy (As-Kd-Qs), meaning my pair of fives is very weak. However, Flashlight (the pre-flop aggressor) checked back the flop, which often indicates they missed or are playing a marginal hand cautiously. While I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a Ten), betting into this board texture is risky because Flashlight's range contains many high cards that beat me. Checking allows me to see a free or cheap river to potentially realize my equity or reach showdown with minimal investment.

The board of Ace-King-Queen-Five is extremely wet and hits the calling range of the Big Blind very hard. My pocket Tens have almost no showdown value here and are effectively drawing only to a Jack for a straight. Betting into this board is unlikely to generate folds from any Ace, King, or Queen, and I would likely be check-raised. Checking behind is the most prudent play to maintain pot control and realize equity cheaply.

On a board of As-Kd-Qs-5c-7c, my pair of fives is extremely weak. However, both the flop and turn were checked around by the pre-flop aggressor (Flashlight), which suggests they likely do not have a strong hand like top pair or better. While I have minimal showdown value, betting into this board is unlikely to force a fold from any Queen, King, or Ace that Flashlight might be checking down. Checking is the most prudent move to see a free showdown or fold if Flashlight bets, as a bet from them on this texture would likely represent at least a pair of Queens or better.

The river has been checked to me. My pocket tens are extremely weak on a board with an Ace, King, and Queen. I have no chance of winning if the opponent has any pair, and betting would only likely be called by hands that beat me. Checking behind is the most prudent action to reach showdown and minimize any further loss while hoping my pair of tens is good against a total air bluff.
I am dealt a very weak, unsuited, disconnected hand (9s 3d) on the button. There is no reason to get involved in the pot with this holding. Folding is the only sensible and standard play.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Seven suited (T7c). The button (Pro) has folded, leaving only me and the Big Blind (Flashlight). T7s is a strong enough hand to open for a raise in a Blind vs. Blind situation to take control of the pot or win it immediately. I have been consistently opening for 300 (3x) in previous hands, and I will maintain that sizing here to put pressure on the shorter stack of Flashlight.

I am in the big blind with Ace-Queen offsuit, which is a very strong hand given the frequency with which my opponent (Flash) has been opening. Since Flash is playing aggressively and I have a significant hand, a 3-bet to 950 is a standard move to build the pot and take control of the hand, potentially forcing a fold or setting myself up for a strong post-flop position if called.

Flashlight has 3-bet to 950, which is nearly a quarter of their remaining stack. While Ten-Seven suited has some post-flop potential, it is likely dominated by the range of hands Flashlight is 3-betting with here. Given that I would be out of position for the rest of the hand and the resulting Stack-to-Pot Ratio (SPR) would be very low (around 1.8), I would have little room for maneuverability post-flop. I will fold and preserve my stack for a more advantageous situation.

I have Ace-Eight offsuit on the button in a three-handed game, which is a strong opening hand. I will raise to 300 to exert pressure on the blinds and take control of the pot in position. This standard 3x open allows me to capitalize on the hand's strength while remaining flexible against potential 3-bets from Flashlight or Pro.

I am in the small blind holding 4h 7c, which is a very weak hand. Calling a raise out of position with such low-ranking, unsuited cards is statistically unprofitable. There is no reason to continue in this hand, so folding is the correct strategic decision.

With AQo in the Big Blind against a Button open, I have a premium hand that is ahead of most of the Button's raising range. 3-betting here builds the pot, takes the betting lead, and can often win the hand uncontested pre-flop. I'll size my 3-bet to 1000 to deny equity and put pressure on my opponent.

I am facing a 3-bet from Pro in the Big Blind after my button open. While A8o is a reasonable hand for a 3-handed game, Pro's 3-bet sizing to 1000 is significant. In previous hands (Hand #44), I have folded A8s to a similar 3-bet, and A8o is weaker. Although I would have position post-flop, A8o often finds itself dominated by better Aces or struggling on many board textures against an aggressive opponent. I will fold here to preserve my stack and look for a more advantageous situation.

I am in the button position with Ace-Deuce offsuit. Being the short stack, I need to look for opportunities to steal the blinds and maintain aggression. An opening raise to 300 is a standard sizing to put pressure on the blinds and potentially pick up the pot pre-flop or take control of the hand in position.

Facing a 3x open from the Button. We have Pocket Fours in the Small Blind. The effective stack against the Button is around 53 big blinds (5325 chips). Set-mining normally requires about 15-20x implied odds; here we are getting ~21x (250 to call for a remaining stack of ~5000). While playing out of position without the initiative can be tricky, the odds are just right to flat-call and try to hit a set, provided the Big Blind doesn't squeeze too often. If we miss our set on the flop, we can easily check-fold.

I am in the big blind with Kh8c. The button (Flashlight) raised to 300, and the small blind (Pro) called. I am getting excellent pot odds (200 to win 700) to call and see a flop. My hand is strong enough to defend in a multi-way pot, especially given the stack sizes and the price. Folding would be too tight, and raising is unnecessary as I am out of position against both players and want to see how the flop develops.

We flat-called pre-flop specifically to set-mine. The flop came 9-6-6, and we didn't hit our set. Playing out of position multi-way with a vulnerable underpair, our best option is to check and look to fold if there is any significant action.

The flop is 9c-6h-6s. I am in the big blind with Kh8c. The small blind (Pro) has checked. The pot is 900. I have two overcards to the board (King and Eight). In a three-way pot, this board is relatively dry, although it favors the pre-flop aggressor or whoever holds a six. Given Pro checked and I have some showdown value/backdoor potential with my overcards, I will check and see what the button (Flashlight) does. This allows me to keep the pot small and avoid committing chips into potentially strong ranges.

I raised pre-flop and was called by both opponents, creating a 900 chip pot. The flop of 9c-6h-6s is a dry, paired board. Having missed the flop entirely with Ace-Deuce offsuit, and with both opponents checking to me, betting into two players on this board is unlikely to generate fold equity against anyone who connected with the nine or a six. Checking behind is the most prudent play to see a free turn card or reach a cheap showdown.

The turn card is perfect for me, giving me a full house (Fours full of Sixes). Since the flop checked all the way through, it's unlikely anyone has a strong hand like trips. If I check again, the turn might check through as well, and I would miss out on value. Leading out with a half-pot bet is the best way to start building the pot and get called by hands like a paired Nine, medium pocket pairs (like 77 or 88), or straight draws (like 87 or 75) that picked up equity on the turn.

On a board of 9c-6h-6s-4d, the small blind (Pro) has led out for 450 into a 900 pot after the flop was checked around. While the pot odds are 3:1, I only hold King-high (Kh8c) with no significant draws and no piece of the board. In a three-way pot, a lead from the small blind into two opponents usually indicates at least a marginal made hand (like a 9 or a small pair) or a 6. My King-high has very little showdown value and minimal equity to improve. Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack.

I am facing a bet of 450 into a 900 chip pot on a 9c-6h-6s-4d board. Having missed the flop completely with Ace-Deuce, my hand has very little showdown value. Pro's turn bet could represent a made hand (a 6 or a 9) or a bet with a pocket pair that he is now turning into a value bet or bluff. With no pair and no draw, calling is a losing proposition, and I should preserve my remaining stack of 5325 chips for a more favorable spot rather than chasing a weak Ace-high hand in a multi-way pot dynamic.
I am on the Button with an extremely weak unsuited hand, 9c3d. This hand has almost zero playability and very poor equity. Folding is the only reasonable choice here.

I am in the Small Blind with 2c4h, which is a very weak hand. The dealer (Pro) has folded, leaving only me and the Big Blind (Flashlight). Folding is the safest and most standard play here to conserve chips.
I am on the button with 2d 8h, which is a very weak hand. Given my stack size and the poor quality of my hand, there is no reason to play it. Standard fold.

Holding Queen-Three offsuit in the small blind is a weak hand, and with no incentive to play out of position against the Big Blind, folding is the most disciplined and conservative play to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with a suited Ace (Ac, 2c), which is a strong hand to open with in late position. Raising to 300 is a standard size that allows me to steal the blinds or take control of the hand in position.

Q8s is a marginal holding to play from the Small Blind facing a 3bb Button open. Given we will be out of position post-flop and the hand can easily be dominated by better Queens or stronger drawing hands, the most prudent action is to fold and preserve our stack.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Seven offsuit (As 7h). The Button (Flashlight) has opened for 300. In a 3-handed game, Ace-high is a strong hand and well ahead of a typical Button opening range. I need to commit 200 more chips to a pot that will be 650, giving me excellent pot odds (roughly 31% equity needed). While I could consider a 3-bet, Ace-Seven plays reasonably well as a calling hand in the Big Blind to keep the opponent's range wide, even though I will be out of position post-flop. Folding would be too tight given the strength of my hand relative to a Button range.

I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor on a very dry board (9-5-2 rainbow). My Ace-high has significant showdown value but is not strong enough to lead out into the raiser. The standard play in this situation is to check to the aggressor and see how they react. If they bet, I will likely call one street given the board texture and the likelihood that my Ace-high is still the best hand or has sufficient equity against their air.

I have bottom pair (deuces) on a dry board. Checking behind is the optimal play here to control the pot size. Betting would likely only get called by hands that have me beat or draw, whereas checking keeps the pot small and allows me to evaluate the turn card safely.

The board has paired on the turn (5h) after a flop that went check-check. My Ace-high (As 7h) still has significant showdown value. Flashlight's check-back on the flop suggests they likely don't have a very strong hand like an overpair or a strong 9, though they could be trapping or holding a medium-strength hand. By checking again, I maintain pot control and allow Flashlight to continue with bluffs or check back weaker hands, taking us closer to a cheap showdown. Leading out now doesn't accomplish much as I'm unlikely to get called by worse or fold out better, except perhaps some hands with equity that might have checked behind. I will check and see how the opponent acts.

I have bottom pair with an Ace kicker on a paired board. Since my opponent checked to me on both the flop and the turn, they are likely either folding or holding a hand with showdown value that they are playing defensively. Betting offers little value as I will likely only be called by better hands or hands that dominate my pair, and checking ensures I get to see the river card for free to potentially realize my showdown value.

The board has run out 5d-9h-2s-5h-6c. I hold Ace-high (As 7h). The pre-flop aggressor (Flashlight) checked back on both the flop and the turn, which strongly suggests they do not have a strong hand like a 9, an overpair, or a 5. Ace-high has significant showdown value in this line. By checking, I am looking to realize that value and see a showdown. If I were to bet, I am unlikely to get called by worse hands, and I would mainly be value-owning myself against small pairs (2s, 3s, 4s) that might have checked along. If the opponent bets after my check, I will evaluate based on sizing, but for now, checking is the most standard play to reach showdown.

I hold a pair of deuces on a board of 5-9-2-5-6. My pair is extremely vulnerable. If I bet, I will almost exclusively be called by hands that have me beat (any 6, 9, or a better kicker). Checking behind allows me to go to showdown and potentially beat any missed draws or worse high-card hands (like Ace-high or King-high) that the opponent might have. There is no strategic value in betting, so checking is the best play to realize my showdown value.

With pocket threes on the Button, I have a clear open-raise opportunity. Raising puts pressure on the blinds and gives me a good chance to win the pot outright or play in position. If I'm called, I can look to set-mine or use my positional advantage post-flop.

I am in the Small Blind holding Ten-Nine suited, a strong speculative hand with great post-flop playability. The Button (Pro) has opened for 250. Folding is out of the question given the strength of my hand. While 3-betting is an option to seize the initiative, calling is a very standard and effective play here to see a flop and utilize the hand's connectivity and flush potential without bloating the pot out of position against a deep-stacked, aggressive opponent.

I am in the big blind with Ace-King offsuit, a premium hand in a three-handed game. The pot is already 600 with the button raising and the small blind calling. Given my stack size is the shortest at the table, I should look to build the pot or take it down pre-flop. A squeeze raise is the optimal play here to isolate one of the opponents or win the dead money immediately. I will raise to 1000 to maximize value with this strong hand.

Flashlight 3-bet to 1000 from the BB after my Button open and a SB call. This represents a very strong range. Calling 750 to set-mine is unprofitable here because Flashlight's remaining stack (4675) does not offer the necessary implied odds (we typically want 10-15x the call amount to profitably set-mine). Furthermore, Flash is still left to act behind me and could easily 4-bet shove. Folding is the most optimal and standard play.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Nine suited, which is a premium suited connector. After my initial call of the Button's open, the Big Blind (Flashlight) has 3-bet to 1000. The Button folded, making it heads-up. I need to call 750 more into a pot of 1500, giving me 2:1 odds. Given the strength and post-flop playability of my hand, these odds are sufficient to continue. Additionally, Flashlight's stack is relatively short (around 4675 total), so the effective Stack-to-Pot Ratio (SPR) post-flop will be low (around 1.6), which simplifies decision-making if I hit a pair or a strong draw.

I have flopped top pair with a Ten kicker on a very dry board (9-5-2 rainbow). In a 3-bet pot, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. Flashlight's range contains many overpairs (TT-AA) that have me beaten, but also many overcards like AK, AQ, and AJ that I am currently ahead of. Given the low Stack-to-Pot Ratio (SPR) of approximately 2.0, I am likely committed to this hand, but checking allows Flashlight to continuation bet their entire range, including bluffs, while keeping the pot manageable if I am behind an overpair.

The flop is 2-5-9 and I hold Ace-King offsuit. I am the aggressor from the pre-flop 3-bet, but this board texture completely misses my range. My opponent check-called a large pre-flop raise, which could indicate a pocket pair or a mid-range connection. Given I have no pair and no draw, betting into a potential made hand is risky. Checking behind allows me to control the pot size and potentially see a turn card to hit an Ace or King while keeping my loss minimal if I am behind.

I have improved to Trip Nines with a Ten kicker on the turn. In the pre-flop action, Flashlight (BB) 3-bet, suggesting a strong range like overpairs (JJ+) or big overcards (AK, AQ). The fact that Flashlight checked back a very dry flop (9-5-2) indicates they either missed completely or are playing an overpair very cautiously. By checking again on the turn, I am maintaining a deceptive line, representing a hand that is ready to fold to pressure. This maximizes the chance that Flashlight will attempt a bluff with their overcards or finally put in a value bet with an overpair like JJ-AA. Given the low SPR, I am looking to get the rest of the chips in, but checking is the best way to induce action from the weaker parts of Flashlight's range.

The board has paired the nine, and having checked the flop, I have yet to connect with any of the board cards. Betting now into an opponent who called a 3-bet is risky as they likely have a piece of this board or a pocket pair. Checking behind allows me to keep the pot small, control the variance, and potentially realize my equity if I improve on the river.

I have trip Nines with a Ten kicker on a board of 2-5-9-9-A. In this 3-bet pot, the pre-flop aggressor (Flashlight) has checked both the flop and the turn, suggesting they either have a marginal showdown hand like a medium pocket pair or overcards that missed. The Ace on the river is a perfect card for me to lead for value. It is strongly in Flashlight's 3-betting range (AK, AQ, AJ), so if they were checking down those hands, they have now improved to top pair and are very likely to call a bet. Additionally, my previous checks make my hand look like a missed draw or a weak pair, which might induce a call from pocket pairs like JJ or QQ that are now suspicious of the Ace. I will bet 1,125, which is exactly half the pot, to maximize the likelihood of being called by their range.

On the river, an Ace paired the board, giving me top pair with top kicker. My opponent, Flash, led out with a bet of 1125. Given that I 3-bet pre-flop and then checked down the flop and turn, my range is capped but contains many broadway cards. A-K is a very strong holding, and folding is out of the question getting 2:1 pot odds. Raising is likely value-towning myself against a random nine or a trapped pocket pair, so I will make the call to showdown.

King-Jack offsuit is a strong opening hand in a three-handed game, especially when on the button. Raising to 300 (3x the big blind) is a standard opening size that allows me to take control of the pot and put pressure on the blinds while remaining consistent with my previous pre-flop raising behavior.

Pocket Jacks are a premium hand, especially in a three-handed game. Being out of position in the small blind against an aggressive opener on the button, it is essential to 3-bet to take the initiative, define the range of the opponent, and build a pot while I have the lead. Raising to 1000 total creates a strong pot and sets me up for a continuation bet on most flops, while protecting my equity against the opponent's aggressive style.

I am in the Big Blind with 8-2 offsuit, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Facing a raise from the Button and a 3-bet from the Small Blind, the only sensible and mathematically sound decision is to fold.

I opened for 3x from the button with King-Jack offsuit, a standard play in a 3-handed game. Flashlight, who is currently short-stacked with only about 25 big blinds, 3-bet to 1000. This 3-bet represents nearly 40% of his remaining stack, suggesting he is committed to the hand. While King-Jack is strong 3-handed, it is frequently dominated by an opponent's 3-betting range (like Ace-King, Ace-Jack, or pocket pairs). Given my significant stack lead and Flashlight's likely desperation after losing a large pot in the previous hand, I prefer to fold and avoid a high-variance confrontation where I would likely be forced to call an all-in on the flop with a marginal holding.

I am on the button with 6d Ts. This is a weak, unsuited hand that does not play well in multi-way pots or even heads-up against the blinds. Given my stack size of 39.5 big blinds, I should prioritize playing stronger hands or waiting for a better opportunity to steal the blinds. Folding is the disciplined play here to conserve my stack.

With 8h 5c in the Small Blind facing a folded Button, this hand is simply too weak and disconnected to play, even for half a blind. Folding is the standard and most profitable decision to avoid playing a trash hand out of position.
8s 5c is a very weak, disconnected offsuit hand. Even from the Button, it is not worth investing chips to attempt a steal or play post-flop. The standard and optimal play is to fold.

I am in the Small Blind with 6c 2d, which is an extremely weak hand. Pro has already folded from the Button. Although it only costs 50 more to complete the bet, 6-2 offsuit has very poor equity and playability post-flop. Given that Flashlight in the Big Blind has a shorter stack and might be defensive or looking for a spot to shove, there is no reason to waste chips on this hand. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am on the button with K4o. While King-high has some value, K4o is generally too weak to open raise from the button in a three-handed game when there's a risk of being re-raised by the blinds. However, looking at the table dynamics, most hands are being won by an open raise pre-flop. That being said, given the stack sizes and current hand count, I will fold this marginal hand and wait for a better spot to utilize my chip lead.

With King-Four suited in the small blind, I have a decent hand to play heads-up against the big blind after the button has folded. Given my short stack size, I want to see a flop cheaply without risking a large portion of my stack, so I will complete the blind.

I am in the Big Blind with a very weak offsuit hand (5s 3h). The Small Blind has just limped in. There is no reason to inflate the pot or try to bluff with such poor equity, so checking to see a free flop is the optimal and standard play.

I have bottom pair (pair of 4s) on a somewhat wet board with potential straight and flush draws. Since I am out of position, checking is the most prudent strategy to control the pot size. Betting here risks bloating the pot with a weak hand, while checking allows me to re-evaluate based on the opponent's action and potentially get to a showdown cheaply.

Flashlight checked the flop after limping pre-flop, showing weakness. I hold 5-high but have an open-ended straight draw (3-4-5-6) using my 3 and 5 with the 4 and 6 on the board. A semi-bluff bet here serves a dual purpose: it can win the pot immediately by forcing out overcards, and if called, I still have 8 clean outs to make a straight. Betting half the pot is a standard, effective size to generate fold equity while managing risk.

The opponent is betting 100 into a 200-chip pot, giving me 2:1 direct pot odds. While my pair of 4s is weak and the board is coordinated with potential straight and flush draws, the small sizing indicates a possible probe bet or a marginal hand. Folding would be too passive, and raising would inflate the pot unnecessarily on a board that hits the big blind's range well. Calling is the most balanced approach to see a turn card cheaply and re-evaluate the strength of my hand.

The board has become extremely dangerous with the turn 7h, creating a straight possibility (any 5 or 9) and completing a three-flush board. My pair of 4s has no showdown value against any serious betting range. As the out-of-position player, checking is the only viable option to control the pot size and prevent committing chips to a likely losing hand. I will evaluate the strength of my hand relative to any potential action from the opponent on the river.

The 7h on the turn gives me an 8-high straight, but the board is now extremely dangerous with four connected cards and three hearts. Any opponent holding a 9 has a higher straight, and anyone with two hearts (or even a high single heart, given the action) could have a flush or a strong draw. Betting here will only get action from hands that beat me or have massive equity against me. Checking behind for pot control is the safest and most strategic play.

The board texture is extremely wet, with four hearts present on the board, completing both potential flush and straight draws. My pair of 4s is essentially a bluff-catcher at this point, with no chance of beating any made flush, straight, or even a higher pair that would have likely bet on the turn. Since the opponent checked behind on the turn, it is likely they also have a marginal hand. Betting here would be a mistake as I would only be called by better hands and it offers no value. Checking is the most prudent action to reach a cheap showdown and potentially win if the opponent is also checking down a weak hand.

The river brings the Ten of Hearts, putting four hearts on the board. I hold the 3 of Hearts, giving me a very weak flush (Ten-high with a 3 kicker). While my hand beats straights, two-pairs, and bluffs, any opponent holding a heart higher than a 3 will beat me. Betting here has no value, as worse hands will almost certainly fold and better hands (any higher heart) will call or raise. Checking behind is the safest and most optimal play to get to showdown cheaply and occasionally win against a non-heart holding.

I am on the button with 4s 5s, a suited connector that plays well in position. With my stack size being significantly lower than my opponents, I need to play proactively to build my stack. Opening with a raise to 300 is a standard move to take initiative and potentially win the blinds or play a controlled pot in position.

I am dealt 72 suited in the Small Blind. This is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Facing a raise from the Button, I will fold, as playing this hand out of position is highly unprofitable.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Nine offsuit. Flashlight (Button) has raised to 300, and Pro (Small Blind) has folded. I need to call 200 more into a total pot that will be 650, giving me roughly 3.25-to-1 odds. T9o is a solid defending hand in the Big Blind against a wide button opening range. Given Flashlight's relatively short stack (35 BB), I want to see a flop and utilize my chip lead without bloating the pot unnecessarily out of position pre-flop.

I have Ten-Nine offsuit on a Jack-high board (Jc 4c 5h). I missed the flop completely. Standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. If they bet, I will likely fold as I have no real equity or draws on this board.

I have flopped bottom two pair (4s 5s on Jc 4c 5h). This is a strong hand, but the board is relatively wet with a flush draw and straight possibilities. I need to bet to extract value from hands like Jack-x, or other pairs, and to protect against potential draws. A bet of 400 into a 650 pot provides good value while forcing opponents with draws to pay to see the next card.

I defended the big blind with T9o against a button raise. The flop came Jc 4c 5h, which completely missed my hand. Flashlight has followed up with a continuation bet of 400. Since I have no made hand and no draws on this board, there is no reason to continue. I will fold and preserve my stack.
JTo is a solid starting hand on the Button in a 3-handed game. I will open with a standard raise to 2.5x the big blind (250) to steal the blinds or play a decent pot in position.

I am in the Small Blind with King-Trey offsuit. The Button (Pro) has opened for 2.5x the big blind. While King-high has some raw equity, K3o is a very weak hand to play out of position against a competent opponent. Furthermore, the Big Blind (Flashlight) is relatively short-stacked and could easily shove over a call. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play in this spot to preserve my stack.
I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Nine offsuit against a button raise. The pot odds are excellent (150 to win 400 total), making this an easy call. I will see a flop and evaluate my position post-flop.

I am in the Big Blind and first to act on a coordinated flop of 8c-Th-Jc. I hold Ah-9d, which gives me an overcard and a double-gutshot straight draw (any 7 or Q completes a straight). Given that I am out of position against the button, checking to see the opponent's action is the most disciplined and standard play to control the pot size while maintaining equity.

I have flopped top two pair (Jacks and Tens) on a very wet and coordinated board (8c Th Jc) that features multiple flush and straight draws. My opponent checked to me, so I should definitely bet for value and protection. I want to charge any draws like club flushes or open-ended straight draws to see the next card, while also getting value from worse made hands like a single pair or weaker two pairs. A bet of around 60-70% of the pot is appropriate to accomplish this.

The board (8c-Th-Jc) gives me an open-ended straight draw (needs a 7 or Q for a straight) along with an overcard (Ace). With 350 required to call a 750 pot, the pot odds are attractive. Calling allows me to maintain my equity and potentially take control on later streets if I hit my draw or if the action slows down. Folding would be abandoning too much equity, and raising as a semi-bluff is risky given my smaller stack relative to the opponent. Therefore, calling is the most balanced and strategic decision.

I am in the Big Blind with an open-ended straight draw (any 7 or Q makes a straight). The board is coordinated with 8-T-J-K, and the King on the turn likely improves my opponent's range. Being out of position, checking to my opponent allows me to control the pot size and see if they check back or bet. If they bet, I can re-evaluate my pot odds, but checking is the most prudent play to maintain my equity without overcommitting with a drawing hand.

The turn brings the Ks, which makes the board extremely dangerous. Any Queen or Nine now completes a straight, and hands with a King like KT or KJ have outdrawn my two pair. My Jacks and Tens are now a marginal hand that mostly serves as a bluff-catcher. Since my opponent checked, betting here would only isolate me against better hands or strong draws, and a check-raise would force me to fold. Checking behind for pot control to see a free river is the safest and most prudent play.

I hold Ace-high on a board of 8-T-J-K-5. The turn checked through, suggesting neither of us has a strong hand, but I cannot beat even the lowest pair (e.g., a pair of 5s). Betting here would be a pure bluff that is unlikely to force a fold from any hand better than mine, as the opponent likely has at least a pair or a missed draw that beats Ace-high. Checking is the standard play to reach showdown cheaply and avoid inflating a pot I am unlikely to win.

The river is a brick (5h), missing the club flush draw. My opponent checked again, indicating they likely do not hold a straight (any Queen or Nine) or a stronger two pair like King-Jack. While I have two pair (Jacks and Tens), betting here is thin and risky because it's hard to get called by worse hands. Busted draws will fold, and a weak single pair might fold to a bet as well. Conversely, if my opponent is trapping with a straight or a better two pair, betting would cost me additional chips. Checking behind allows me to safely realize my showdown value.

I am on the button with K3 offsuit. While Kx hands are often worth an open in three-handed play, K3o is at the bottom of the range. With the Small Blind (Flashlight) sitting on a 35BB stack and the Big Blind (Pro) being the chip leader, I prefer to fold this marginal hand and wait for a better opportunity rather than risking a 3-bet or playing a weak hand out of position post-flop.

I am in the small blind with a very weak hand (5-4 offsuit). Given my stack size is significantly shorter than my opponent's, playing a weak hand out of position against the big blind is strategically unsound. It is better to conserve my stack for a more favorable situation.

I am on the button with Ace-Seven offsuit. My stack is relatively short at 3500, so I need to be proactive to accumulate chips. Raising to 300 is a standard button open to attempt to steal the blinds or take initiative in the hand.

Facing a 3x button open from Flashlight, QJo is a marginal offsuit broadway hand. From the Small Blind, we are out of position and vulnerable to a squeeze from the Big Blind or domination from the Button's opening range. It has negative expected value to call and is too weak to 3-bet for value, while 3-betting as a bluff is risky against a 35bb stack. Folding is the most disciplined and optimal play.

I am in the Big Blind with K3o. Flashlight (the button) has raised to 300, and the Small Blind has folded. Flashlight is the short stack with about 32 BBs remaining. While K3o has some high-card value, it is a very weak hand to defend with out of position, even given the pot odds. It doesn't flop well and is easily dominated by the button's opening range. Since I have a comfortable stack and am currently in second place, I will choose the low-variance route and fold to wait for a better spot.
Ten-Deuce suited is a very weak hand, even from the button. There is no reason to put chips into the pot with this holding. Folding is the optimal play.

I am in the Small Blind with 2h7s, which is the weakest possible hand in Texas Hold'em. The button (Pro) has folded. It costs me only 50 to call, but this hand has almost no post-flop playability and Flashlight is a relatively short stack compared to the rest of the table. Completing the small blind here is generally a losing play with 72o as I will be out of position for the rest of the hand with the worst starting cards. Folding is the standard and correct play.

I am on the button with Queen-Jack suited, which is a premium broadway hand. Standard strategy from the button is to open-raise to put pressure on the blinds and seize the initiative. Flashlight in the small blind has a shorter stack (around 36 BBs), so I need to be aware of potential 3-bet shoves, but QJh is strong enough to open and potentially call a shove from a short stack depending on the odds, though against a 3-bet from Pro I might have to be more cautious. I will stick to my standard 3x open-raise of 300.

I hold an Ace-high hand in the small blind against a button raise. My stack is relatively short compared to my opponents, so while I need to play aggressively to build my stack, I should not overcommit with A-6 offsuit. Calling is the standard play here to see a flop in position against an active player, maintaining pot control while defending my blind.

We are in the Big Blind with 9c5c. The Button raised 3x and the Small Blind called. Playing a heavily gapped, weak suited hand multiway and out of position is a recipe for losing chips, as we suffer from reverse implied odds and rarely flop strong enough to continue. Folding is the most profitable play.

I am in the small blind with bottom pair (6s) and an Ace kicker on a T-T-6 board. Since I called the pre-flop raise, checking to the original aggressor is the standard and most prudent play. This allows me to gauge their strength or potentially see a free turn card if they check behind, especially given my relatively short stack of 3400.

I have two overcards (Queen, Jack) and backdoor straight and flush draws. I have two pair (Tens and Sixes) on a dry board. With a stack of 3400 and a 1000 chip pot, I am in a strong position. Calling the 300 bet is the best play to keep Flash's potential bluffs in the pot and maintain pot control, rather than raising and potentially forcing them to fold their weaker hands.

I am in the small blind holding a pair of sixes (6c) with an Ace kicker on a T-T-6-4 board. My hand is a bluff-catcher or a marginal showdown hand, but certainly not strong enough to lead out into the button aggressor. Checking allows me to control the pot size, potentially induce a bluff if Flash has air, or simply see the river for free if they check behind.

The turn 4d is essentially a blank. Flashlight checked again after calling my continuation bet on the paired flop. My Queen-high has some showdown value and two overcards that could improve to the best hand on the river. Given Flashlight's shorter stack (around 3100 remaining), betting here risks a check-raise that would force me to fold my equity. Checking back allows me to control the pot size, see a free river, and potentially win the hand if Flashlight is also holding overcards or a missed draw.

The board is T-T-6-4-7, and I hold A-6 for two pair (Tens and Sixes) with an Ace kicker. While this is a decent hand, it is vulnerable to any pocket pair higher than sixes, any Ten, any Seven, or a straight (like 5-8). Betting here would likely only be called by better hands that have me beat. Checking is the most prudent play to reach showdown and control the pot, as my hand functions best as a bluff-catcher against my aggressive opponent.

On the river, the board is Td-Th-6s-4d-7h. Flashlight has checked for the third time (pre-flop call, flop call, turn check, river check). Flashlight has a relatively short stack, and after calling the flop, they might be sticky with any pair or Ace-high. Given that the pot is already decent and I have a huge stack, I'll take the free showdown and hope my Queen-high is somehow good or simply minimize losses if it's not. Checking is the most conservative and safest play here to preserve my chip lead.

With a short stack of 4,400 chips in a three-handed game, I need to play aggressively to accumulate chips. 3c9c is too weak of a suited hand to play from the Small Blind facing an open raise from the Button. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Three offsuit. The button (Flashlight) has raised to 300. I am on the Button with Ace-Deuce suited. This is a solid hand to open with in late position. I have blockers to strong Ace hands and the potential to draw to the nut flush. Raising to 2.5x the big blind is my standard sizing to pressure the blinds.

I am in the Small Blind with K2o. The Button (Pro) has raised to 2.5x. Flashlight in the Big Blind is also short-stacked and could potentially shove over a call. Given the tournament stage and my healthy stack, there is no need to defend with such a weak hand out of position.

I am in the Big Blind with 8-2 offsuit, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Despite the decent pot odds to call the 150 chips, I am out of position against the Button (Pro) for the rest of the hand. Playing a weak, dominated hand like 8-2 offsuit in a raised pot out of position is a losing strategy long-term. Folding is the disciplined choice.

I am on the button with 6-3 offsuit, which is one of the weakest possible starting hands. Given the current tournament situation and the fact that I've been playing quite a few hands recently, there's no merit in trying to steal the blinds or play this hand post-flop. Folding is the standard and correct play here to preserve my stack.

With the button folding and me in the small blind, I am heads-up against the big blind. My hand, Queen-Jack offsuit, is a strong holding in this position. Given that I am the short stack, I need to play aggressively to accumulate chips and maintain pressure. A raise to 200 is a standard, effective play to take control of the hand, potentially winning the pot pre-flop or setting up a continuation bet on the flop.

Facing a min-raise to 200 from the Small Blind, I am getting excellent pot odds (100 to win 300) to call from the Big Blind. Ten-Nine offsuit plays reasonably well post-flop, and having positional advantage will allow me to maneuver effectively. I will flat call to see a flop.

The flop of King-King-Four is very dry and favors the big blind's calling range, as they are likely to have a King or pocket pairs in their range. My Queen-Jack high has very little equity here and is essentially a bluff catcher at best. Betting into this board would likely just be called or raised by any King, which beats me. The board of Ks 4s Kh strongly misses both of our ranges, but is paired, making it hard to float without any equity. With ten-nine offsuit, I have no pair and no meaningful draw. Betting here as a pure bluff with zero equity is unnecessary given I have position and can just take a free card to see if I improve on the turn or if the opponent gives up completely.

The board remains very dry with a paired King and a Three. My Queen-Jack high has no showdown value and likely loses to any King, any pair, or even just Ace-high if my opponent has it. Since my opponent checked the flop, they could be playing a weak range or trapping. Betting into this board does not accomplish much as I will likely be called by any hand that is beating me. Checking is the most prudent play to control the pot size, see a free river, and keep the losses minimized with a weak hand.

Flashlight has checked both the flop and the turn, signaling a capped and weak range—likely Ace-high, Queen-high, or a low pocket pair that is wary of the paired Kings. With Ten-high and no draw, I have zero showdown value. However, this is a prime opportunity to take the pot away with a delayed bluff. A half-pot bet here applies pressure and should fold out many of the better high-card combinations that currently beat me.

The turn bet from the opponent, after a passive check-check on the flop, suggests they have either connected with the board or are taking a stab at the pot. My Queen-Jack high has zero showdown value and no meaningful equity against even a weak pair. Continuing with no hand and no draw is not a profitable strategy. Folding is the disciplined choice here to preserve my stack.

With J-4 offsuit on the button, this is a clear fold. I have 42.5 BBs remaining and need to wait for a stronger hand to steal the blinds or contest a pot, as this specific hand has very poor equity and post-flop potential against two larger stacks.

We are in the Small Blind with 9s 7c, an offsuit two-gapper. With the Button folding, we are facing the Big Blind. 97o is too weak to play profitably out of position, even for a completion. Folding is the standard and safest option here.

Ace-Ten offsuit is a strong starting hand to open from the Button in a 3-handed game. I'll make a standard raise to 2.5 big blinds to steal the blinds or play the pot in position if either opponent calls.

I am in the Small Blind with Qs7c, which is a weak hand to play out of position. Pro (the button) has opened for 250. I am in the Big Blind with 3-5 offsuit, which is a very weak hand. Even though I am getting odds to call, playing this hand out of position against a button raise is negative expected value. Folding is the disciplined choice.

I am on the button with Nine of Diamonds and Five of Spades. This is a very weak starting hand that does not play well post-flop. Given the stack sizes and the tendency of both the Small Blind (Flashlight) and Big Blind (Pro) to play back at raises, there is no reason to open-raise with a bottom-tier hand. With Q2o in the big blind facing a small blind limp, my hand is too weak to raise for value or as a bluff, as it plays poorly post-flop. Checking allows me to see a free flop in position and evaluate from there without risking unnecessary chips.

I am in the small blind with bottom pair (4s) on an Ace-high board. This is a very difficult board for my hand range. Checking is the most standard and defensive play here to control the pot size and avoid value-betting myself against any Ace or better pair that the Big Blind might hold. If the opponent bets, I can re-evaluate, but leading into this board with a weak pair is suboptimal.

On an Ace-high rainbow flop with just Queen-high and no draws, checking behind is the safest play. There is no need to commit chips on a bluff when I have very little equity if called, and I can realize my free equity by seeing the turn card.

I have bottom pair on an Ace-paired board. My hand is extremely marginal and has very little showdown value against almost anything the opponent might have. Since the opponent checked the flop, it's possible they don't have an Ace, but there's no reason for me to bloat the pot with this strength. Checking is the standard play to keep the pot small and reach the river to see if I can get to a cheap showdown or fold if the opponent shows strength.

On the turn, the board pairs the Ace. Flashlight checks again, showing weakness. While a stab might fold out King-high or weak bottom pairs, my Queen-high has some inherent showdown value against missed broadway cards or random connectors. Checking keeps the pot small, avoids unnecessarily bloating the pot with a weak hand, and allows me to see a free river card where I can re-evaluate.

My hand (3h 4h) has improved to a full house (Fours full of Aces) with the board of A-A-8-4-4. Checking is the most prudent play to safely reach the showdown and avoid potentially losing more chips.

On the river, the board is double paired (Aces and Fours). My Queen plays as a strong kicker (A-A-4-4-Q), which beats many missed random hands like Jack-high or Ten-high that Flashlight might have limped with. Betting here would likely only get called by a King-high, an 8, or better, and folding out worse hands has no value. Checking behind is the safest play to realize my showdown value without risking extra chips.

I am holding a 2-9 offsuit, which is a very weak starting hand with little potential for strong combinations. As the shortest stack at the table, it is imperative to play conservatively and avoid committing chips to marginal spots. Folding here preserves my remaining 4250 chips for a better opportunity.

Holding Ten-Three offsuit in the Small Blind, I have an extremely weak hand. Even though it is a cheap call or a potential steal situation, T3o plays terribly post-flop and has very low equity against the Big Blind's range. The most +EV decision is to fold and wait for a better starting hand.

K2o is a weak, disconnected offsuit hand. Even on the button, it is generally too weak to open-raise, especially with a shorter stack in the Big Blind who might defend aggressively or shove. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here.

I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Trey offsuit. The Button (Pro) has folded, leaving only me and the Big Blind (Flashlight). I am on the button with K d 4 s. In a three-handed game, King-high on the button is a standard opening hand. Raising helps to potentially take the blinds immediately or put pressure on the opponents while having position post-flop. A standard 3x open to 300 has been my consistent sizing throughout the session.

I am in the small blind with a very weak hand, Queen-Three offsuit. Calling a raise from the button out of position with such a hand is negative expected value. It is best to fold and preserve my stack for a more favorable situation.

Facing a 3x open raise from the Button, I hold 8s5s in the Big Blind. This is a suited two-gapper which is too weak to defend out of position against a standard opening range. The correct play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

With Queen-Five offsuit in the button position, this hand is far too weak to commit chips, particularly given my relatively short stack of 4,250. Folding is the most disciplined strategy to preserve my chips and wait for a more premium holding to capitalize on my button position.

Ah Th is a strong premium hand, especially in a blind vs blind scenario after the Button folds. Raising to 3x (300) will build the pot, take the initiative, and likely get folds from weaker hands or value from worse Aces and broadway hands that the Big Blind might call with.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Jack suited (KJs), which is a very strong hand in a blind-versus-blind scenario. Since the Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 300, I have the advantage of being in position for the remainder of the hand. While calling is an option to keep the pot smaller, 3-betting allows me to take the initiative and extract value from a wide Small Blind opening range. I will raise to 950 to put pressure on Pro's range and potentially take the pot down immediately or build it for a post-flop advantage.

ATs is a strong starting hand, especially in a blind-versus-blind scenario. While facing a 3-bet from the Big Blind is concerning, ATs has great equity and playability post-flop. A 4-bet could force out bluffs and isolate us against premium hands, so flat-calling is the standard play. It keeps the opponent's range wide while allowing us to see a flop and realize our equity.

I have bottom/middle pair (Ten) with top kicker and an overcard on a highly coordinated board that heavily favors the pre-flop 3-bettor's range. As the out-of-position caller, checking to the pre-flop aggressor is the standard and most sensible play. Leading out (donk betting) would only isolate me against stronger hands and bloat the pot unnecessarily.

I have flopped the nut straight (K-Q-J-T-9) on a very coordinated board. I have middle pair with top kicker on a highly coordinated, wet board (9s Qc Tc) that heavily favors my opponent's pre-flop 3-betting range. Opponent combinations like overpairs (AA, KK), top pair (AQ, KQ), sets (QQ, TT, 99), and strong draws (AK, AJ, or club flush draws) completely crush my hand. I don't have any straight or flush draws to improve my equity, meaning I'm drawing very thin against their value range and only slightly ahead (or flipping) against their high-equity bluffs. Facing a half-pot continuation bet out of position without a clear path to improve, folding is the most fundamentally sound decision.
Queen-Three offsuit is a very weak starting hand, even from the button. It lacks the playability and raw equity needed to open-raise profitably. Folding is the standard and correct decision here.

Action is folded to me in the Small Blind. Queen-Nine offsuit is a strong enough hand to raise for value and to put pressure on the Big Blind in a blind-vs-blind situation. Flashlight (BB) has a shorter stack (approx. 41 BBs) and has recently been folding to pre-flop aggression. A standard 3x raise is appropriate here.

I am in the big blind with Queen-Four suited. The small blind (Flash) has raised to 300. With a pot of 400 and a call of 200 required, I am getting 2:1 odds. While the hand is not premium, it is suited and in position for the post-flop play. Given the stack size, I have enough chips to see a flop and re-evaluate, especially against an opponent who has been opening frequently.

I have flopped top pair with a decent kicker (9) on a board of Th-2h-Qc. The board is somewhat wet with flush and straight draws available. Since I was the pre-flop aggressor and have a strong made hand, I will place a continuation bet for value and to protect my hand against various draws in Flashlight's range. A bet of 350 into the 600 pot (roughly 58% of the pot) is a standard sizing to extract value from hands like JT, T9, or heart draws.

I have flopped top pair (Queens) and have a flush draw with the four of hearts, which gives me significant equity against most of the opponent's range. Calling the 350 bet is the optimal play to keep the pot size manageable while waiting for the turn, where I may hit my flush or improve my pair, and I maintain the ability to re-evaluate if the board texture changes unfavorably.

The Turn card (Jack of Hearts) is quite coordinated and potentially dangerous. It completes several draws (straights and flushes) that Flashlight could realistically have in his range after calling the Pre-Flop raise and the Flop C-bet. While I still have Top Pair (Queens) and have picked up an open-ended straight draw (any King or Eight), the board is now very wet. Checking is the most prudent play here to control the pot size. If Flashlight checks behind, I get a free card to try and hit my straight or see a safe river. If Flashlight bets, I can re-evaluate based on the sizing, but checking prevents me from being blown off my equity by a potential semi-bluff or a completed hand from a relatively short-stacked opponent.

I have completed a Queen-high flush with the 4h, Qh, Jh, Th, 2h on the board. Since my opponent checked the turn, this is a prime opportunity to extract value from their range. I will place a bet of 700 into the 1300 pot to build the pot size and capitalize on my strong hand.

I have top pair (Queens) and have picked up an open-ended straight draw (any King or Eight) on the turn. Although the Jack of Hearts is a scare card that completes potential flushes and straights (like K9 or 98), I am getting favorable pot odds of nearly 4:1 (700 to win a total pot of 2700) to call. My hand still has significant equity given the straight draw and the possibility that my top pair is still the best hand against Flashlight's semi-bluffs or lower two pairs. Flashlight is relatively short-stacked, and calling allows me to see a river card without committing my entire stack immediately in a situation where I might be behind.

QTo is a solid starting hand on the button in 3-handed play. A standard raise to 2.5x (250) puts pressure on the blinds and allows us to take the initiative with positional advantage.

Flash 3-bet pre-flop and fired a small continuation bet on the flop, but his turn check often signals weakness, such as missed overcards (AK/AQ), a flush draw, or a medium pocket pair (77-99) trying to pot control. With pocket Tens, I have a strong overpair. Betting here serves a dual purpose: it extracts value from smaller pairs and flush draws, and it crucially denies free equity to his overcard combinations that could hit on the river. By sizing around 40-45% of the pot, I charge his draws an appropriate price while maintaining pot control, and I can comfortably fold to a check-raise if he happened to be trapping with JJ+.

Pre-flop, I 3-bet Ace-Jack suited and led the flop for a continuation bet on a 6-5-6 board. Pro called, indicating they likely have a piece of this board, such as a six, a five, a pocket pair, or a straight/flush draw. The turn 4h further connects the board, potentially completing a straight for 78 or improving hands like 56 and 4x. After I checked the turn to control the pot, Pro bet 1200 into 2900. While I have Ace-high and two overcards, I am out of position on a highly coordinated board that heavily favors the range of a player who calls a 3-bet (which includes many small/medium pairs and suited connectors). With only 11 hands remaining in the tournament and a lead in chips, I will choose to fold rather than call a significant portion of my stack with just Ace-high on a board where I am likely behind or facing a high-equity draw.

In a heads-up situation with blinds at 50/100, Six-Four suited is a playable hand from the button. I will put in a standard raise to 300 to maintain aggression and potentially take down the blinds or see a flop with initiative. This has been my consistent opening size throughout the match.
T4o is a very weak hand, even in a heads-up scenario. Against a 3x pre-flop raise from the button, the best move is to fold and wait for a better spot to invest chips.

I am in the Small Blind/Button with 6s 9h, which is a very weak, disconnected offsuit hand (a 3-gapper). Given the stack sizes are very close and we are nearing the end of the match, there is no need to play speculative or trash hands out of position or even in position. Folding is the standard and most EV-preserving play here.

I am on the button in a heads-up situation with Js 6c. While Js 6c is a marginal hand, in heads-up play, the button should be opening a very wide range of hands to maintain pressure and take advantage of position. I will put in a standard raise to 300. If my opponent 3-bets, this hand is weak enough to fold without much loss, but if called, I have position for the rest of the hand.

9s7h is a weak, offsuit, gapped connector. Defending a 3x open raise from the Big Blind with this hand out of position is unprofitable and will typically lead to tough spots post-flop. Folding is the correct choice here.

Q8s is a solid suited hand heads-up. Raising from the button takes the initiative and puts pressure on the big blind. A standard 2.5x open to 250 is effective to steal the blinds or play a bloated pot in position.

I am in the Big Blind with T4o. Pro has raised to 250 from the Button (Small Blind). Calling 150 more to see a flop with a 3.5:1 pot odds (150 to win a total pot of 500) is tempting, but T4o is a very weak hand that plays poorly post-flop, especially out of position. Against a competent opponent like 'Pro' in a heads-up or 3-handed dynamic, I prefer to fold these bottom-tier hands to avoid tricky post-flop situations given the stack sizes. I'll wait for a better spot to utilize my stack advantage as we approach the end of the 150-hand limit.

I am on the button with T3s. In a heads-up situation with a stack slightly larger than my opponent's, I have the option to complete the small blind, raise, or fold. Given that there are only a few hands left in the tournament and T3s has some playability but is not a strong raising hand against 'Pro' who has been active, completing the small blind for an additional 50 chips is a reasonable way to see a flop cheaply while maintaining control.

I have Ace-King offsuit in the Big Blind, which is a premium starting hand. Flash has simply limped from the Small Blind. I want to build the pot, extract value, and punish the limp since my hand is significantly ahead of their limping range. I'll raise to 4x the big blind (400) to take control of the hand pre-flop.

I am in position (Button/Small Blind) and have been raised to 400 after my initial limp. With 10d 3d, I have a suited hand that can flop well. Although the kicker is weak, the pot odds are roughly 1.6:1 (calling 300 to win a pot of 800 total), and playing in position for the rest of the hand gives me a significant advantage. With only 7 hands remaining and a slim lead, I'll call to see a flop and look for opportunities to outplay my opponent or hit a strong draw/pair.

The flop comes 6-7-2 rainbow, missing my Ace-King, but my pre-flop aggression gives me the range advantage, representing all the strong overpairs. A small continuation bet of around 1/3 to 1/2 pot should fold out his high-card hands and missed floats that have equity against me. If called, I still retain six outs to hit top pair on the turn or river.

On a flop of 6s-7d-2h, my Ten-high with a backdoor diamond draw is quite weak. Pro, who raised pre-flop, has made a small continuation bet of 300 into a pot of 800. While the price is relatively attractive for a float in position, I have very little equity and few favorable turn cards. With only 6 hands remaining in the tournament and a slim lead, preserving my stack and avoiding a potential large loss with a marginal hand is the most prudent strategy. I'll fold and look for a better spot to utilize my chip lead in the final few hands.

T7o is a weak offsuit hand, and while I have positional advantage post-flop as the Button/Small Blind, it is generally not profitable to play such marginal hands. With a slight chip lead late in the match (Hand 145/150), folding and preserving my stack is the optimal and lowest-risk decision.

At Hand 146 of 150, I am heads-up against Pro with nearly identical stacks. Holding 6-3 offsuit on the Button (Small Blind), I am looking at one of the weakest hands in the deck. While I generally want to play a high percentage of hands in heads-up play, 6-3 offsuit is not a hand worth defending or raising with, especially given Pro's recent aggressive play. With only five hands left in the tournament, I will fold this hand to preserve my stack and look for a better spot to gain a lead in the final four hands.

A5o is a solid hand to open-raise from the button in heads-up play. Having an Ace blocks some of the opponent's strongest continuing ranges, and we have decent equity against most defending hands. A standard 2.5x open is my typical sizing to pressure the big blind.

I hold Jack-Ten of Spades, a premium suited hand that plays very well. Pro has opened from the button/small blind. Given the tournament is nearing its end (Hand 147 of 150) and our stacks are nearly equal, I want to assert dominance and take the initiative. Since I will be out of position post-flop, a 3-bet to 850 is a strong play to either win the pot immediately or define the range of the hand while holding significant equity.

While A5o is a perfectly fine hand to open from the button in heads-up play, it does not fare well when facing a 3-bet. Against a standard 3-betting range, A5o is often dominated by stronger Aces and big pairs, and it plays relatively poorly post-flop since it rarely flops better than a weak one-pair hand. Furthermore, with only a few hands left in the tournament and a slight chip lead, there is no need to bloat the pot or take unnecessary high-variance risks. Folding is the most solid and mathematically sound choice here.

I am on the button with Ace-Eight offsuit. With only three hands remaining in the tournament and nearly even stacks, Ace-high is a strong starting hand. I will put in a standard raise to 300 to exert pressure on the Big Blind and attempt to take the pot pre-flop or define the hand early. This has been my consistent opening size and remains effective.

With 7c Th in the Big Blind against a 3x raise from the button, my hand is far too weak to defend. Out of position and with very low equity, the most profitable and standard play is to fold and preserve my stack for a better opportunity, especially with only a few hands remaining in the match.

With T8o in the Small Blind, the hand is too marginal to aggressively open, especially against an opponent who has shown a willingness to 3-bet. Given there is only one hand remaining after this, I prefer to preserve my stack and look for a better spot in the final hand of the tournament rather than committing chips out of position with a weak holding.

This is the final hand of the 150-hand session. I currently have a chip lead of 550 (15,200 vs 14,650). My hand, 9-Trey offsuit, is extremely weak. Since my goal is to maximize my stack and secure my position relative to my opponent, there is no reason to risk a significant portion of my chips in the final hand with a subpar holding. Folding costs only the 50 chips already committed as the small blind, ensuring I finish the tournament with a lead and a stack of 15,150.
